section_text: This player is a strategic, oscillating free-rider who alternates between full cooperation and full defection rather than using intermediate contributions. They use only the extremes of the action space: either keeping everything or contributing the entire endowment, with no partial amounts, suggesting a simple, rule-based heuristic rather than fine-tuned optimization. Contribution levels are volatile over time but follow a clear conditional pattern. When they observe the group in a highly cooperative state (many peers making high or full contributions), they are willing to fully join the cooperative norm and contribute everything. When group cooperation looks weaker or more mixed, they revert to pure free-riding. They do not appear to punish low contributors through their own behavior in a sustained way; instead, they opportunistically exploit cooperative episodes and withdraw support when cooperation wavers. They are highly responsive to the current social environment: their cooperative bursts line up with periods where most others are contributing generously. This implies a belief that cooperation can be temporarily profitable or normatively compelling when many others comply, but that there is no obligation to keep contributing once the group’s effort slackens. That is, they practice conditional cooperation with an opportunistic, self-serving tilt. The player shows no evidence of strong fairness norms in the sense of maintaining their own contribution to match others’ average over time. They are comfortable taking large benefits during phases when others contribute much more than they do. However, they are not a pure, unyielding free-rider: they periodically shoulder a large cooperative burden by contributing fully when they perceive the environment as “worth it” or normatively salient. Risk tolerance appears moderate. Contributing everything is risky in the sense that, if others defected, the payoff would be poor. The fact that they sometimes choose full contribution suggests they accept this risk when signals of group cooperation look strong. Conversely, they do not “experiment” with small contributions to probe the group; instead, they switch sharply between extremes, implying a coarse-grained evaluation: either “this group is cooperatively safe, so I go all in” or “this is not worth it, so I give nothing.” Overall, this player is best described as a conditional, opportunistic cooperator who prefers to free-ride when group cooperation is moderate or fragile, and to contribute fully when cooperation appears robust and collectively beneficial.

This player is a predominantly low contributor who intermittently makes medium or high contributions when others are clearly sustaining high cooperation. Across the game, they more often keep the full endowment than not, indicating a primary incentive to maximize individual payoff rather than to consistently support the public fund. Their pattern is conditional rather than random: when the group’s average contribution is high and widespread cooperation is visible, they sometimes shift to contributing a substantial portion of their endowment, occasionally even the full amount. When contributions from others drop or become more uneven, they revert to zero. This suggests that they recognizes the efficiency of the public good and is willing to participate when they perceive a reliably cooperative environment, but they are not willing to be a “pillar” of cooperation when others seem less committed. Contribution levels show a limited range of voluntary sacrifice. They move between no contribution, partial contributions roughly around half the endowment, and, more rarely, full contributions. The willingness to sometimes give up the whole endowment, but only in cooperative contexts, indicates moderate risk tolerance with strong reliance on social cues: they take the “risk” of contributing more only when free-riding risk appears low because many others are contributing heavily. They does not appear to enforce or follow a strict fairness norm like “match the group average” or “always give half.” Instead, they behaves more like a cautious opportunist: they exploits cooperative periods by sometimes free-riding and sometimes joining in, and they systematically exploit low-contribution periods by not contributing. There is no evidence that they tries to lead the group toward higher cooperation; rather, they adapts to it when it exists and withdraws support when it weakens. Overall, their cooperation is unstable and reactive. They is capable of cooperative behavior but does not internalize a firm norm of consistent fairness. Their default is self-interested low contribution, softened by occasional conditional cooperation when the environment looks very favorable.

This player is a predominantly unconditional high contributor with occasional, deliberate deviations. Their default is to give the maximum possible amount and they sustain this pattern through essentially the entire game, despite observing repeated under-contribution by at least one peer. This indicates a strong prosocial or efficiency-oriented motive that is not easily undermined by free-riding. Their contribution pattern is highly stable and predictable: maximum giving is the norm, while reductions are rare, discrete events rather than the start of a downward trend. These few low-contribution choices appear exploratory or retaliatory tests rather than a full strategy shift. Across the game, they repeatedly return to the maximum-contribution baseline immediately after such deviations, suggesting that high cooperation is their reference point. They exhibit some conditionality: they do occasionally free-ride or partially free-ride when others’ contributions drop or when the group has recently shown reliability. However, these episodes are sparse and short-lived. The player seems to be checking (a) how much personal gain is possible from defecting and (b) whether the group’s cooperation will collapse in response. Once they confirm that others remain largely cooperative, they revert to full contributions. This indicates that they value both payoff and group efficiency but prioritize sustaining cooperation over systematically exploiting it. Normatively, they appear to hold a strong fairness and efficiency norm that “everyone should give fully.” Their behavior is consistent with someone who treats full contribution as the socially appropriate baseline and tolerates some deviation by others without abandoning that norm. When they do deviate, they do not push the system toward full collapse; instead, they test the margin and quickly re-align with the high-contribution convention. This suggests they see themselves as a cooperator first, not as an opportunistic free-rider. Risk tolerance is moderately high in a social sense. Continuing to fully contribute even when a free-rider or partial free-rider is present exposes them to exploitation in the short run. They still take that risk, implying trust in the group’s overall cooperative orientation and/or a focus on maximizing total group returns rather than strictly securing their own relative advantage. At the same time, their rare defection episodes show they are not naive: they are aware of the payoff structure and are willing to temporarily prioritize self-gain, especially when they anticipate that group cooperation is robust enough to survive it. In summary, this player is best characterized as a “principled high contributor with opportunistic tests”: they default to full cooperation, strongly support a high-contribution norm, occasionally probe the limits of that norm through brief free-riding or partial contributions, then reliably return to maximal giving once the test is complete.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional high contributor. They treat full contribution as the default and maintain it across almost all rounds, even when others occasionally reduce their input or free-ride. Deviations from full contribution are rare, modest, and short-lived; they do not escalate into a downward spiral of defection. The pattern suggests a strong internal norm favoring maximal cooperation rather than a finely tuned, round-by-round response to others. Stability is high: the player repeatedly returns to the maximum contribution after brief dips. This indicates a robust commitment to a simple rule (“contribute fully”) rather than experimentation or fine-grained payoff optimization. Their occasional reductions in contribution appear more like mild probes or momentary frustration than the beginning of a strategic shift; they quickly revert to full cooperation once they see others still largely contributing. They display conditionality, but it is soft and asymmetric. Severe free-riding by one or two group members does not trigger large or sustained retaliation; at most, it prompts a small, temporary reduction in their own contribution. They seem willing to bear some exploitation to preserve overall group efficiency, indicating a social preference that prioritizes maintaining a cooperative equilibrium over strict reciprocity. Their fairness norms are strongly pro-social and group-oriented. They act as if the “fair” behavior is that everyone contributes the maximum, and they live up to that standard themselves even when others occasionally fall short. They do not appear to chase individual short-term advantage and tolerate some inequality in their own disadvantage if the group remains largely cooperative. This is consistent with a preference for collective welfare and a reluctance to initiate or amplify breakdowns in cooperation. Risk tolerance is high with respect to being exploited. The player repeatedly chooses the strategy that is most vulnerable to free-riding (full contribution) despite observing that some others occasionally deviate. They appear willing to risk lower individual earnings in order to stabilize a high-contribution norm. They do not seem risk-seeking in a random or volatile way; rather, they accept a stable, principled risk of exploitation as the cost of adhering to a cooperative rule. Overall, they behave like a principled, norm-driven cooperator who is only mildly and briefly responsive to others’ lapses and who keeps anchoring the group at high contribution levels.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-sensitive conditional contributor with substantial volatility. Contributions cluster in a mid-to-high range rather than at full free-riding or full cooperation, but fluctuate notably from very low to full endowment. The player often contributes amounts around or somewhat above the emerging group average, but does not consistently match the highest cooperators. When group contributions appear weaker or mixed, the player is willing to lower their own contribution substantially; when the environment looks more cooperative or strategically salient (e.g., when others’ average is high or cooperation seems fragile), the player is willing to raise contributions, including occasionally giving the full endowment. This suggests conditional cooperation rather than a fixed rule. The player does not appear to follow a simple monotone “end-game defection” pattern; instead, contributions remain in a middle-to-high range even near the end, and can spike or drop in ways that appear responsive to the current social context rather than only to the remaining horizon. They accept earning less in some periods when they contribute more, indicating that they value supporting the public good and/or sending a normative signal, but they are not purely altruistic: at other points they clearly cut contributions down to preserve private payoff when cooperation seems unrewarding or uncertain. Risk tolerance is moderate. The player is willing to “risk” high contributions in settings where many others are contributing strongly, but they also hedge by reverting to modest or low contributions when cooperation looks less stable. Fairness norms appear present but not rigid. The player behaves as if low contributors should not be allowed to benefit unchecked; their contributions tend to be higher when the rest of the group is contributing a lot, but they are also prepared to pull back and partially free-ride when they perceive others as exploiting the group or when the environment becomes noisy or less cooperative. Overall, the contribution pattern fits a “conditional cooperator with enforcement tendencies”: they prefer a cooperative norm, are willing to support it materially, but dynamically scale their own giving based on how strongly they believe others are upholding that norm.

This player fits a “profit-focused opportunistic cooperator” type. They understand early that contributing is individually costly given the low multiplier and generally aim to maximize their own payoff, not group efficiency. They alternate between zero, low, and occasional high contributions rather than following a stable rule, suggesting responsive but self-centered adaptation rather than a principled norm. They clearly free-ride in many situations where others contribute heavily, keeping their entire endowment while still benefiting from the group pot. They also show that they are willing to contribute substantially when it seems psychologically or strategically salient (for instance, when others are all in, or after a period of freeriding), but this is not sustained. The pattern suggests they are not purely selfish automatons: they sometimes join cooperative surges or give a moderate amount when the group is moderately cooperative. However, they never lock into consistent high contributions, and quickly revert to low or zero when they can still benefit from others’ efforts. Conditionality is present but weakly pro-social. They contribute positive amounts more often when others are generally high contributors, and they are willing to jump to a large or very large contribution when everyone else also appears fully cooperative. At the same time, they repeatedly free-ride on a deeply cooperative core of other players, showing low concern for proportionality or fairness in the long run. They do not attempt to “match” others’ average contributions; they are comfortable contributing significantly less than the group while still accepting the benefits. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They occasionally “risk” a full or large contribution into a highly cooperative environment, but otherwise they stick to safer, individually favorable choices. They do not experiment much with intermediate patterns to test group reactions; rather, they respond in a coarse way: all-in sometimes, small token contributions sometimes, and zero in many rounds. Their decisions suggest that the dominant motive is exploiting cooperative structure for personal gain, tempered by occasional bursts of conformity or maybe mild guilt, but with no sustained fairness-based strategy.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-always-full contributor with a single late-game deviation. They almost always contribute their entire endowment to the public fund, including in early uncertainty, mid-game when others defect, and late-game when many are tired or cynical. Their contributions are only weakly conditional on others’ behavior: they keep contributing fully even when multiple others free ride, and they return to full contribution immediately after their one defection. The brief defection appears more like experimentation, fatigue, or a momentary payoff grab than a shift in norms. They demonstrate a strong fairness norm favoring “everyone all in,” articulated explicitly in chat, and they understand the efficiency of full cooperation. Risk tolerance is high: they consistently expose themselves to exploitation by low contributors rather than protecting their personal payoff. Over time, there is no systematic downward trend, so they do not “learn to defect”; instead, they maintain a prosocial, cooperation-first strategy and treat non-cooperation as something to be nudged via communication and incentives rather than imitated.

This player is a strongly cooperative high contributor who treats full contribution as the norm as long as others broadly do the same. Their default is the maximum possible contribution. They maintain this even when one group member persistently contributes nothing and another sometimes contributes less than the maximum, suggesting that they prioritize group efficiency over strict payoff maximization and tolerate some degree of free‑riding. They are conditionally cooperative in a mild, forgiving sense: occasional or moderate deviations by others do not lead them to reduce their own contribution. Only very late in the game, when several others reduce their contributions and the horizon is almost over, do they themselves step down from full contribution. This indicates both a concern for fairness and an awareness of diminishing returns to being the lone high contributor when end‑game incentives change. Their behavior suggests relatively low risk aversion with respect to being exploited: they continue to fully cooperate even after being the only one maintaining full contributions in the presence of a clear, repeated defector. They appear motivated by efficiency and norm consistency more than by securing a strictly superior individual payoff each round. Overall, they fit a “principled, efficiency‑oriented conditional cooperator” type: they cooperate robustly, stay the course in the face of moderate exploitation, but adjust when the environment shifts and when cooperation from others becomes less reliable near the end.

This player is a payoff‑oriented conditional cooperator with opportunistic free‑riding. They understand that cooperation is profitable in this environment (the public return per contributed coin exceeds 1), but they do not internalize a strong fairness or reciprocity norm. Their contribution levels fluctuate between full contribution, partial contribution, and zero. The pattern suggests they track how generous the group is and periodically exploit high group contributions by contributing nothing while still benefiting from the public pool. When they see widespread full cooperation, they are willing to free‑ride rather than match others’ efforts. They also show no clear tendency to systematically contribute more when others contribute more; instead they mix cooperative and selfish moves without a clear tit‑for‑tat pattern. They appear relatively insensitive to individual deviations by low contributors; they are willing to cooperate even in the presence of visible free‑riders elsewhere in the group. Conversely, they are also willing to defect even when everyone else cooperates. This indicates that their primary reference seems to be their own payoff maximization rather than matching the group’s average or enforcing equality. The overall strategy balances extraction and participation: they contribute enough across the game to maintain access to a thriving public fund but exploit windows where they can greatly increase their private payoff by contributing nothing. They tolerate inequity in their own favor without apparent discomfort.

This player is a high, prosocial contributor who stays above group average and close to the maximum feasible level. Contributions are relatively stable across rounds with only a mild downward adjustment, suggesting conditional cooperation rather than blind altruism. They appear to follow a fairness norm where strong contributors—especially those at or near the top—are the reference point. Individuals contributing clearly less than the strong contributors are tolerated rather than sanctioned, indicating a cooperative but non‑moralistic stance. They seem willing to give up substantial private payoff to support the public good despite the relatively low marginal per-capita return, implying low payoff-maximizing selfishness and higher weight on group efficiency and/or norm adherence. Risk tolerance regarding being exploited is moderate: they do not immediately drop to low contributions despite heterogeneity in others’ behavior, but they also do not push to full contribution throughout, suggesting they balance idealism with cautious adjustment.

This player is a moderately prosocial but payoff-conscious contributor. They almost always give a positive, mid-range amount, typically near or slightly above the emerging group average. They rarely exploit low-contributing environments by dropping to minimal contributions, but they also do not move to the very highest levels even when many others do so. Their behavior suggests a “conditional cooperator with a floor”: they appear willing to support the public good, but only within a band that maintains their own payoffs. Contribution levels adjust modestly in response to changes in group generosity, but not in a highly volatile or extreme way; they remain in a narrow range rather than oscillating between full free-riding and full cooperation. They show no clear evidence of applying strict fairness or matching rules toward specific others; their pattern fits more with reacting to the general contribution climate and guarding against being too far above it. Risk attitude toward cooperation seems moderate: they repeatedly invest in the public good even when others free-ride, but they do not “push” cooperation aggressively by moving to maximal contributions.

This player fits a “quiet, stable free-rider with minimal cover” type. They almost always contribute the smallest nonzero amount that plausibly avoids standing out, with occasional zero contributions. Their contributions are low and remarkably stable over time; they do not show meaningful upward adjustment even when others repeatedly argue for full cooperation and many players do contribute high amounts. A single mid-game deviation to a larger contribution is isolated and not followed up; it does not signal a real strategy shift. Their behavior is strongly conditional on others in an exploitative way: when many others cooperate heavily, they maintain or even drop to very low contributions, thereby reaping high personal payoffs. They do not reciprocate generosity, even when cooperation among others becomes quite strong and repeated. They also do not appear to punish defectors by increasing their own contribution when others drop; instead, they simply continue their low contributions regardless. They do not express fairness norms or concern for equality in the transcript. Their pattern indicates that they prioritize personal payoff over group efficiency, even when the group clearly tries to coordinate toward efficient, high-contribution outcomes. They tolerate free-riding on highly cooperative others and appear comfortable maintaining that position despite explicit social pressure against it. Risk tolerance appears low with respect to cooperating: they show little willingness to “take the risk” of matching high contributors, even after others demonstrate repeated prosocial behavior. However, they show comfort with social risk (being suspected as a “bot” or freeloader) as long as it does not lead to material consequences, which in this setup it does not. Overall, they exhibit a stable, payoff-maximizing free-rider strategy, with modest cosmetic variation (oscillating between 0 and a token 1) rather than true conditional cooperation.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They consistently allocate their full endowment to the public good, showing no adjustment in response to changes in others’ behavior, average contributions, or the presence/absence of rewards to them. They tolerate free-riding and moderate contributions from others without reducing their own level, suggesting either a strong internal norm of “always cooperate” or a deliberate choice to ignore short-term payoff optimization. Their behavior fits a high-idealism / low-conditionality type rather than a conditional cooperator: they do not appear to condition on others’ contributions or on being rewarded. They exhibit high social-risk tolerance: they keep contributing fully even when many others clearly deviate downward, and they do not hedge against possible exploitation. The pattern is consistent with a “principled cooperator” who values maintaining contribution norms for their own sake, or believes that stable full cooperation is best for the group and behaves accordingly regardless of strategic incentives.

This player is a “conditional learner-cooperator with slow start.” They initially free-ride when the environment is still uncertain, then rapidly shift to full cooperation and remain stable once they infer that high contributions are the emerging norm. After the first decision, their pattern is simple and consistent: they always contribute fully when they expect most others to do the same. They appear sensitive to both group behavior and peer messaging. When others demonstrate strong cooperation and at least one player explicitly explains that contributing yields higher returns, this player aligns with that advice and does not revert to defection later. They do not appear to enforce strict reciprocity on individual defectors; they keep contributing fully even while a few others persistently contribute nothing. This suggests that their cooperation is guided more by a desire to follow the apparent group strategy and gain from the public good than by a personal fairness rule that punishes every non-cooperator. Risk tolerance is moderate: they are initially cautious (keeping their full endowment) until they see strong cooperative signals, then they are willing to “risk” full contributions for the remainder of the game. Across the game, they show low volatility after the first adjustment and do not experiment further, consistent with a satisficing strategy: once convinced that “everyone giving” pays, they stick to it.

The player is a pure free‑rider. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute to the public fund, despite being in a relatively high-return environment for cooperation. This pattern is completely stable; there is no experimentation with contributing, no conditional reciprocity, and no apparent response to fluctuations in others’ contribution levels. They tolerate receiving substantial benefits from others’ cooperation while remaining uncooperative themselves, indicating weak or absent fairness concerns toward the group. Risk tolerance regarding payoff variance appears low: they choose the individually safe, dominant strategy of zero contribution rather than seeking higher joint payoffs through mutual cooperation.

This player is a mostly high-contribution, but strategically opportunistic and occasionally deceptive cooperator. They usually match the group’s norm of full contribution when a clear consensus for maximizing the public fund emerges, and they demonstrate that they understand and accept the efficiency of full cooperation. However, they intermittently defect by contributing nothing (and once a partial amount) while others mostly cooperate, and they do so without announcing any change in strategy or tying it to others’ misbehavior. Their pattern is not purely conditional on average group behavior: they defect even in periods when almost everyone else is contributing the maximum, and they later return to full contribution while those same others continue cooperating. This suggests a willingness to “skim” extra payoff when they believe they will not be punished and group cooperation is strong enough to sustain high returns despite their free-riding. They show some instability: long stretches of maximal contributions are punctuated by abrupt zeros or low contributions without explanation. The timing suggests opportunism rather than confusion: they have already demonstrated comprehension of the rules and of the effectiveness of full cooperation, and their defection coincides with situations where others remain cooperative. Their fairness norm appears self-serving: they endorse and benefit from the cooperative norm, but they do not adhere to a strict reciprocity or equality principle, instead tolerating asymmetric gains in their own favor. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they defect a handful of times in a highly communicative environment where free-riders are noticed and criticized, but they do not defect persistently. This pattern is consistent with calibrated risk-taking—extracting extra benefit occasionally while avoiding becoming an obvious chronic defector.

This player is a steadfast full cooperator. They always contribute the entire endowment and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or reduce their contributions. Their behavior is highly stable and non‑volatile across the game. They appear to understand that maximal contribution is individually profitable given the parameters (implied by their repeated insistence on going “all in” and acceptance of the high multiplier) and treat full cooperation as both the efficient and morally correct baseline. They do not condition their own contribution on others’ previous behavior; instead, they behave as an unconditional cooperator in practice. They tolerate substantial inequality in favor of free‑riders without retaliating or experimenting with lower contributions. This suggests low willingness to sacrifice group welfare to discipline defectors, and a strong commitment to the cooperative norm once adopted. Their risk tolerance with respect to others’ opportunism is high: they repeatedly expose themselves to exploitation for the sake of preserving the cooperative outcome.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximal contribution, never experimenting with free-riding or partial cooperation even when others reduce their contributions or seem inattentive. Their strategy prioritizes maximizing group payoffs over short‑term individual gain, despite the marginal per‑coin return being less than 1. They appear to anchor on the simple rule “everyone contributes fully, every time” and do not revise this rule in response to occasional defections, exits, or accidents. They treat full contribution as both a moral and efficiency norm: contributing maximally is “what we do” and is also the way “we all get more.” They maintain this pattern even when it is clear that there are intermittently selfish or absent players, suggesting relatively low sensitivity to exploitation and high tolerance for minor inequities as long as the group as a whole stays mostly cooperative. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation (e.g., adjusting their own contributions contingent on others’ behavior). Instead, they behave like a steadfast norm enforcer via talk, not via changing contributions. They are risk‑accepting in the sense that they consistently invest their entire endowment into the public pool, trusting others to keep cooperating, and they do not hedge even later in the game when boredom and dropout become salient. Fairness for them is primarily “everyone chips in fully” rather than fine‑grained reciprocity; as long as that broad pattern holds, they are satisfied and encourage continuation.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximum contribution, never deviating despite occasional free-riding or accidents by others. Their behavior suggests they internalize the efficiency logic of full contribution and stick to it, even when others temporarily reduce contributions or when the group composition changes. Their contributions do not appear contingent on short-term fluctuations in others’ behavior; they continue to give the full amount through both high- and lower-payoff phases. This indicates low sensitivity to mild social dilemmas and an absence of retaliatory or “teaching” motives. They neither attempt to punish by withholding nor to strategically free-ride near the end, implying weak strategic opportunism and strong preference for consistent cooperation or for minimizing cognitive effort in decision adjustment. Fairness norms seem to be present but not behaviorally enforced via contribution changes. When others lag or free-ride, the player does not respond by reducing their own contribution; instead, they sustain contribution while expressing mild frustration or impatience in chat. This pattern is consistent with someone who values group efficiency and possibly the simplicity of a fixed rule (“always give full”) more than fine-tuned conditional cooperation. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in social terms: they repeatedly expose themselves to exploitation risk (always giving full contribution) and do not hedge even when some others are clearly not fully reliable. They seem more concerned with moving the game along and securing the predictable cooperative payoff than with optimizing against potential defectors. Overall, their strategy is: pick the socially efficient, high-cooperation action and stick with it throughout, regardless of noise, boredom, or occasional selfish play by others.

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional high contributor once coordination around full cooperation is established. They quickly move from a slightly cautious but generous contribution to effectively full contributions and then maintain maximal giving with no signs of backsliding. Their behavior stabilizes at the cooperative norm very early and remains extremely consistent, with no experimentation, no strategic under-giving, and no apparent attempts to exploit others’ generosity. They appear strongly conditional on the emergent group norm but not on precise fairness adjustments. After observing that others are willing to contribute very generously, they align to that standard and then fully match the group’s cooperative pattern. Once they see that “everyone goes all in,” they treat that as the equilibrium and act accordingly. There is no evidence that they track small deviations by others or adjust in response; they act as if maintaining a simple, high-contribution rule is sufficient. Their fairness norm seems group-focused rather than individualistic: they are comfortable contributing at or near the maximum as long as others are broadly doing the same, and they do not show any concern about fine-grained payoff equality or about slightly over- or under-contributing relative to the group in the early phase. They behave as if the important thing is that the group achieves and keeps a fully cooperative state, not that their own share is perfectly fair each round. Risk tolerance is high in social terms: they are willing to commit fully to cooperation once they have a basic indication that others are inclined to cooperate. They do not hedge against potential future defection, and they do not slow-walk contributions upward over time. Instead, they jump to full cooperation and stay there, even though they cannot see how many rounds remain or other players’ full outcome details. This suggests they prioritize maintaining a clear cooperative strategy over guarding against potential exploitation. They follow a simple strategy: “once cooperation is working, always contribute everything.” They do not appear to test alternative contribution levels, seek to optimize narrowly, or react to small deviations. Adaptation is front-loaded: after initially responding generously but not perfectly maximally, they quickly converge to full contributions and then stop adapting, as if they believe the optimal pattern has been found. Overall, they are a stable, norm-following, high-trust cooperator who strongly favors and sustains efficient, group-beneficial outcomes.

This player is a highly cooperative, near “all-in contributor” type who quickly aligns with a group contribution norm and then sticks to it with strong stability. After initially agreeing in chat to a full-contribution strategy, they almost always contribute the maximum possible amount and do not experiment with low contributions or opportunistic free-riding once the group norm is clearly cooperative. The one early non-maximal contribution suggests they are not mechanically fixed to “always-max,” but rather are willing to adjust once they observe others’ behavior. Their contributions become fully stable once it is clear that others are consistently cooperative. They appear strongly conditional on the perceived group standard: when others advocate and then demonstrate all-in behavior, this player matches and maintains it. They do not retaliate against early low contributors nor try to undercut the group later; instead, they behave as a loyal cooperator who values sustaining the high-contribution equilibrium over short-term individual gain. Fairness norms seem important: the player behaves in line with a “we should all do the same, high” standard and never attempts to exploit others’ trust. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in the pro-social direction. They are willing to risk being exploited by potential defectors, especially early, once they have verbally committed to cooperation. They do not hedge or gradually increase contributions; they rapidly move to a fully cooperative profile and stay there when the environment remains favorable. There is no evidence of payoff-maximizing deviation at the end, suggesting limited focus on endgame exploitation and a stronger emphasis on maintaining group harmony and predictable mutual gains.

This player is an uncompromising, stable full cooperator in this environment. They always contribute their entire endowment and never test lower amounts, even when others deviate early on. Their behavior suggests they prioritize maximizing total group payoffs once they recognize that the game’s structure makes full contribution individually profitable. There is no evidence of conditional or retaliatory cooperation; they contribute fully regardless of earlier free-riding and do not adjust in response to others’ changes. They appear comfortable with the strategic risk of cooperating despite initial defects, indicating either confidence that others will align or a willingness to accept short-term vulnerability for potential long-term gains. Their fairness norm seems to favor uniform full contribution across all players; they do not complain about early lower contributions by others, but their steady behavior aligns with a strong expectation that “everyone should put everything in.” They show no opportunism toward the end of the game and do not attempt to exploit the group once strong cooperation is established. Overall, they fit a “principled efficiency-maximizer” profile: once they see that cooperation is both socially and individually beneficial, they lock into the cooperative optimum and remain there.

This player is a moderate, self-regarding cooperator with stable but below-average contributions. They rarely give nothing, but also avoid high contributions that match or exceed the group norm. Their typical pattern is to contribute a small to mid-range portion of the endowment and keep a substantial private share. They appear sensitive to the general contribution climate: when peer contributions look broadly cooperative, they are willing to contribute in the low-to-mid range; when they likely perceive themselves as being targeted or exploited, they are willing to drop to very low or zero contribution. They do not chase “full cooperation” levels even when many others appear highly cooperative, suggesting that maximizing group efficiency is not their main priority. Instead, they seem to balance some willingness to cooperate with a clear concern for their own payoff and for not being the “sucker” who contributes much more than low contributors. Their behavior is relatively stable over time, with only a few clear adjustments downward that look like reactions to punishment or perceived unfairness. Overall, their fairness norm seems asymmetric: they accept being slightly below group average and appear more concerned with avoiding personal loss or humiliation than with strict equality or full provision of the public good. Risk tolerance in contributions is moderate: they do not experiment with extreme highs or sustained free-riding, but they do occasionally test lower contributions once the social environment feels hostile.

This player fits a “calculating conditional cooperator / opportunistic free-rider” type. They do not have a stable contribution level; instead they vary contributions widely, including using the full range from full free-riding to full endowment. This indicates high volatility and active experimentation rather than a fixed norm or habit. They clearly recognize and exploit the incentive structure. Their behavior shows that they understand they earn more by contributing less than others when others are contributing substantially. They repeatedly choose low or zero contributions while surrounded by many high contributors, which reliably gives them above-average payoffs. They are therefore willing to free-ride when they expect others to maintain the public good. At the same time, they are not a pure selfish optimizer in the narrow sense. They are occasionally willing to contribute moderate or high amounts, including once contributing the entire endowment. This suggests they do not hold a strict “never contribute” rule. Instead, they appear to condition their behavior on the recent history of group cooperation and on payoff comparisons. When the group appears cooperative and payoffs from the pool are strong, they sometimes match that with moderate or high giving; when they see that low/zero contributions still yield strong personal payoffs, they revert to minimal giving. There is no clear evidence that they are following a simple “match-the-average” heuristic or strict reciprocity; their individual choices sometimes move opposite to the emerging contribution trend. This implies a focus on optimizing individual payoff under uncertainty more than on maintaining a fairness standard. Any fairness concerns, if present, appear weak and easily overridden by payoff considerations. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high. They are willing to make stark shifts in contribution (including fully free-riding) even in a group where many others are contributing heavily, suggesting comfort taking advantage of others’ efforts despite the risk that the group might collectively reduce contributions later. They also briefly make a maximal contribution despite not being able to see how many rounds remain, indicating a willingness to risk short-term sacrifice, possibly to test whether higher cooperation can be sustained or to see the impact on payoffs. Overall, they are adaptive and payoff-sensitive, with a tendency toward strategic opportunism rather than stable cooperation or stable defection.

This player is a generally high, prosocial contributor with occasional moderate downward deviations. They typically either give the full endowment or a large majority of it, indicating a strong cooperative baseline and comfort with bearing individual cost for group benefit. Their contribution pattern is not purely mechanical: at several points they reduce their contribution to a mid-range level when group contributions have recently been lower or more uneven, then return to high cooperation when others’ behavior improves. This suggests conditional cooperation anchored to a personal “high but not blind” fairness standard rather than strict equality matching. They tolerate being the highest contributor in the group at times, so they do not require perfect reciprocity, but they are sensitive to pronounced free-riding or sudden drops by others and briefly adjust downward in response. The variability is limited and never reaches full defection, which points to a reluctance to exploit others even when their own previous high contributions were not fully reciprocated. Overall, they combine a cooperative orientation with mild, situational downward adjustment rather than a sharp strategic switch.

This player is a strong, almost unconditional cooperator. They contribute the full endowment in essentially every round, including the very last one, despite multiple opportunities to free‑ride profitably. The only deviation is a single early round in which they contribute nothing while the entire group also withholds; they then immediately revert to full contributions and maintain that pattern stably. Their cooperation does not depend on others’ short‑run behavior: they continue to give fully even when some peers free‑ride and when cooperation occasionally collapses. This indicates a stable internal norm of full contribution rather than a reactive “tit‑for‑tat” or purely payoff‑maximizing strategy. Risk tolerance is relatively high: they are willing to keep investing in the public good despite uncertainty about others and despite having experienced punishment themselves. Concern for fairness is oriented toward “everyone should cooperate fully,” but they do not adjust their own contribution downward when others violate that norm.

This player is a moderately volatile conditional cooperator who mixes sincere contribution with opportunistic free-riding. They do not settle into a stable “always cooperate” or “always defect” pattern; instead, they oscillate between low, medium, and full contributions. Across the game, they both fully free-ride and fully contribute multiple times, indicating flexibility rather than a fixed rule. They clearly respond to others’ behavior. When the group appears generously cooperative, they sometimes exploit the situation by contributing nothing while benefiting from the public fund. At other times, in similarly cooperative environments, they match or exceed typical contributions, suggesting they are not a pure egoist but are willing to sustain high provision some of the time. They also contribute meaningfully in environments where others contribute less. In such states, they still put in nontrivial amounts instead of uniformly dropping to zero, which points to at least a partial internal norm of “doing their share” even when the immediate monetary return on each contributed coin is low. However, this norm is not rigid: they are willing to revert to taking everything for themselves when it is profitable and the group looks especially cooperative, showing that fairness considerations coexist with payoff-seeking. Their contribution levels span the full range (none, partial, and all), and they switch between these levels several times. This indicates experimentation and sensitivity to context rather than consistent adherence to any single fairness rule (such as “always match the group average” or “always split evenly”). They seem to accept substantial risk to their own per-round payoff when they make large contributions, especially in less cooperative contexts, implying at least moderate tolerance for outcome variability and a willingness to “test” whether cooperation is reciprocated. Overall, this player behaves like a payoff-conscious conditional cooperator: they value mutual cooperation and sometimes act as a strong contributor, but they are quite ready to free-ride on others’ generosity when the environment looks favorable and unpunished. Their guiding principle appears to be a shifting balance between reciprocity, experimentation, and opportunistic self-interest, rather than a fixed fairness or efficiency norm.

This player is a calculated, alternating cooperator–defector whose primary objective is to maximize personal payoff while keeping the public-good mechanism viable enough that others continue to contribute. They reliably use both available actions (full contribution or full defection) rather than sticking to a single mode. Their pattern shows: - Frequent switches between contributing and free-riding rather than a monotone trend, indicating active experimentation and conditionality rather than a fixed rule. - Willingness to cooperate even when others’ average contributions are modest, which suggests they understand that the multiplier makes group contribution socially efficient and are not purely short-term myopic. - Equally, repeated episodes of defecting when others are contributing at high levels, taking advantage of the public good. This shows comfort with exploiting cooperative environments when it is clearly profitable. - No strict matching rule (e.g., they do not just “copy” the modal behavior): sometimes they contribute when many defect, and sometimes they defect when most cooperate. This implies they track payoffs and strategic advantage more than simple reciprocity. - Fairness norms appear weak or highly instrumental: they tolerate large payoff advantages over others without any corrective behavior and also accept relative disadvantages when cooperating if they see future or aggregate benefit. They seem more payoff-oriented than inequality-averse. - Risk tolerance is moderate: they are willing to “bet” on cooperation even when others are partially free-riding, but they do not lock into a high-cooperation stance; instead, they periodically pull back to defection to secure higher private returns. Overall, this is a payoff-sensitive, opportunistic conditional cooperator who mixes cooperation and defection to balance personal gain against the danger of collapsing the public good.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-conscious contributor with some conditionality but no clear push toward full free-riding or full altruism. They keep their contribution at a stable, intermediate level for much of the game, rarely matching the highest contributors even when many others give a lot. They also do not drop to zero when others defect, instead trimming their own contribution modestly. This pattern suggests they value contributing to the public good but want to maintain a relatively favorable private payoff and avoid being the “sucker” who contributes the most. They appear somewhat responsive to the general contribution climate: when group contributions are high on average, they are comfortable remaining a mid-level contributor rather than exploiting heavily; when others’ contributions weaken, they reduce their own somewhat, but not to the lowest levels seen in the group. Their behavior is consistent with a cautious conditional cooperator who benchmarks around an intermediate standard rather than matching the group’s top or bottom. The player shows no evidence of strongly enforced fairness norms (e.g., they do not systematically match the group mean or anchor on equal-contribution ideals); instead, they occupy a relatively self-protective but not aggressively exploitative middle ground. Risk tolerance in contribution appears moderate: they are willing to put nontrivial amounts into the public good even when some group members defect, but they do not escalate to high contributions to “save” the group.

This player is a mixed, somewhat erratic cooperator who alternates between full cooperation and full free-riding. They never choose intermediate amounts, only the extremes allowed. Their pattern does not settle into a stable rule like “always match the majority” or “always defect after being punished”; instead they switch several times between contributing everything and contributing nothing. They sometimes contribute fully even when others mostly free-ride, and sometimes free-ride when others contribute, suggesting weak or inconsistent adherence to fairness norms. They do not appear to prioritize payoff maximization in a straightforward way: they willingly choose full contribution in situations where it is clearly individually costly and socially unrewarded. Their risk tolerance regarding potential exploitation is moderate to high, as they are repeatedly willing to expose themselves to being the sole or minority contributor without securing reciprocal behavior from others.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, never experimenting with holding back, even when several others free-ride or cooperation temporarily collapses. Their behavior indicates a strong internal norm that full contribution is the “right” or default action, not something contingent on others’ immediate choices. They show high tolerance for payoff risk: they keep investing fully into the public good despite visible drops in group efficiency and persistent free-riding by a subset of players. They do not appear to condition their cooperation on punishment activity or on the presence/absence of deviations; they maintain the same cooperative choice under both perfect cooperation and mixed cooperation environments. Their fairness orientation is therefore cooperative but non-conditional: they prioritize maximizing the group’s potential surplus rather than adjusting to maintain payoff equality. Learning or strategic updating in contribution behavior is absent or minimal; they do not test lower contributions even when there are repeated defections from others.

This player is a near-unconditional high cooperator. They almost always contribute the full endowment and rarely deviate. The only clear deviations are a single low contribution and one intermediate contribution early on; both are brief and not repeated. Overall, the pattern is: start fully cooperative, experiment once or twice with imperfect cooperation, then settle into consistently maximal contributions even when one or two others keep free riding. They do not appear to condition their own giving tightly on others’ behavior. Even when a persistent low contributor continues to benefit, they keep putting in the maximum. They show no evidence of trying to “match” others’ contributions or of downshifting in response to unfairness. This suggests a strong internal norm of contributing rather than a payoff-maximizing or reciprocity-based strategy. Risk tolerance seems moderate to high in the social sense: they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by free riders and to potential disapproval from punishers rather than hedge by lowering contributions. They do not visibly seek to protect themselves against punishment by exerting extra conformity beyond already being a perfect contributor. Fairness-wise, they seem to interpret fairness primarily as “everyone should put in a lot,” but they enforce this only through their own behavior, not through sanctions.

This player is a volatile, strategically flexible contributor who alternates between full free-riding and full contribution rather than settling at a stable level. They do not display a consistent intrinsic preference for always cooperating or always defecting. Instead, their pattern suggests responsiveness to social context and perceived detection or norms: after an initial choice that exploits the group, they are willing to switch to full cooperation, then revert to free-riding later. This indicates conditional cooperation shaped more by perceived incentives and social pressure than by a fixed fairness norm. Their willingness to fully free-ride despite strong social signals favoring contribution suggests relatively low inequity aversion and a tolerance for appearing exploitative when they believe it is advantageous or safe. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to take social risks (defecting when others may notice) but also willing to conform to cooperative behavior when the social environment seems to demand it. Motivation seems mixed: they value personal payoff but are also sensitive to being observed and judged, adjusting contributions accordingly.

This player is a fully unconditional cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no deviation across the game and no visible sensitivity to others’ occasional non-contribution. They behave as if full cooperation is the default and correct action, independent of short-term payoffs or free-riding by some peers. There is no sign of strategic withholding, conditional cooperation, or retaliation against low contributors. Their risk tolerance for being exploited by free-riders is high; they accept that some others do not contribute and continue to invest fully in the group outcome. Fairness, in their behavior, is defined more by supporting a high public good than by matching others’ individual contributions.

This player is a strict unconditional cooperator under the available actions. They always contribute the full endowment to the public fund and never deviate, regardless of how others behave or how the group average fluctuates. There is no observable conditionality on freeriding, partial cooperation, or instability in the group. They appear to accept personal risk of exploitation without attempting to protect themselves via reduced contributions. This suggests a strong internal norm that “the right move” is to cooperate fully, independent of short‑term payoff considerations or observed minor defections. Fairness, if present as a concern, is not enforced through contribution adjustments; instead, they tolerate some inequality in order to preserve high provision of the public good.

This player is a steadfast full cooperator. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund and never deviate, regardless of others’ behavior, reward patterns, or fluctuations in group contribution levels. Their cooperation is stable rather than reactive: they do not adjust when some others free-ride or when public good provision is lower. This suggests an internalized cooperative norm or rule-based strategy rather than outcome-based optimization. They appear tolerant of inequality; they continue to contribute fully even when others contribute less and even though this leaves them vulnerable to exploitation. There is no evidence of risk-sensitive experimentation or conditional cooperation; they do not “test” lower contributions, and they do not appear to match or condition on the group’s average. Their behavior is consistent with a principled or duty-oriented cooperator who prioritizes maximizing group output independent of short-term personal payoffs.

This player is a generally high contributor who aligns with a strong cooperative norm. After an initially moderate contribution, they reliably give the maximum endowment in most periods, even when some others give little or nothing. Their behavior indicates a preference for sustaining group efficiency rather than maximizing short-term private payoff. They do not appear highly risk-averse regarding being exploited; they continue to cooperate despite repeated personal losses from being punished and from others’ low contributions. There is one notable deviation where they sharply reduce their contribution well below the emerging group norm; this suggests their cooperation is conditional and can temporarily withdraw when they feel wronged or perhaps when “testing” the group. However, they quickly return to full contribution, implying that their baseline identity is as a cooperator rather than a persistent defector or an erratic player. They seem to hold a fairness norm that high contributions are expected from all, and they are willing to personally bear costs (via punishment) to enforce that norm.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor who consistently gives the maximum possible amount in every round. They do not appear to condition their contribution level on others’ behavior, on punishment/reward incentives, or on where they are in the game horizon. Their behavior indicates a strong internal norm for full cooperation rather than a strategic reaction to fluctuating group contributions. They tolerate the risk that others may free-ride and do not scale back even when low contributors persist, suggesting that maintaining a cooperative standard matters more to them than short-term payoff optimization. Fairness seems to be defined as “everyone should contribute fully,” but their own contribution does not depend on whether this standard is met.

This player is a mostly high contributor with sharp, strategic deviations. They are initially eager to coordinate on full cooperation and explicitly endorse “all in” contributions. Their default seems to be maximal contribution when they expect the group to follow the cooperative norm. However, they are willing to switch to zero when they either (a) test defection as a profitable option or (b) react to the evolving punishment environment. Their pattern shows responsiveness to others’ behavior rather than a fixed rule: they alternate between fully cooperative and fully free-riding choices, suggesting conditional cooperation with opportunistic elements. They do not appear risk-averse in payoffs, as they both cooperate and defect in ways that expose them to potential punishment. Concern for fairness is present but not strongly internalized; they support the efficient cooperative outcome in words and behavior early on but later exploit cooperation when it seems individually advantageous or when norms weaken. Overall, they are a “conditional cooperator with opportunistic defection,” not a pure altruist or pure free rider.

This player is an unwavering unconditional cooperator. They always contribute the full endowment, with no variation, even when others occasionally free-ride. Their cooperation does not appear to depend on short-term payoff optimization; they forgo the higher private payoff of defecting in favor of sustaining full-group efficiency. They exhibit a strong fairness norm that everyone should contribute fully, but they do not condition their own contribution on others’ behavior. Risk tolerance is high with respect to exploitation: they continue to cooperate fully despite clear evidence that some others sometimes defect, suggesting either a principled commitment to cooperation, a desire to model “correct” behavior, or both. There is no sign of strategic under-contribution near the end; horizon considerations do not weaken their cooperative stance.

This player is a strict, unconditional full cooperator under the given binary choice (all-or-nothing contribution). They always contribute the maximum allowed amount, with no deviations across the entire interaction. Their behavior does not adjust to changes in group cooperation, even when others repeatedly free-ride and earn substantially more. This indicates that they either prioritize the collective outcome or follow a rule-based or principle-driven strategy rather than payoff-maximization. They do not exhibit conditional cooperation (no evidence of “matching” or “responding” to others). Their pattern remains the same whether others mostly contribute, mostly defect, or alternate. They tolerate systematic exploitation by a persistent free-rider without reducing their own contributions, suggesting low sensitivity to being disadvantaged relative to others, or a very strong internal norm to keep contributing regardless. They appear willing to accept a clear monetary disadvantage in order to maintain full cooperation. This is consistent with a high level of social preference for group welfare, adherence to a fairness or “do my part” norm independent of reciprocity, or possibly a misunderstanding of the payoff structure (uncertain). However, the stability of contributions across different group compositions suggests that their default heuristic is “always contribute,” not a mis-click or random noise. Overall, their contribution pattern is highly stable, non-reactive, and strongly prosocial in a narrow sense (they always invest in the public good) even though it is individually costly and not rewarded by others.

The player is a steadfast, pure free-rider. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute, despite consistently observing high and sustained cooperation from most others. Their behavior is extremely stable: no experimentation, no conditionality, and no visible learning or adaptation. They appear to prioritize maximizing private payoff over group efficiency or fairness norms, even when it is clear that others are sacrificing to support the public good. The player does not reciprocate cooperative behavior and does not shift strategy in response to moral appeals, group norms, or changes in others’ contributions. Risk tolerance regarding group outcomes is high: they are willing to let others bear the cost of maintaining cooperation while they extract the benefits. There is no indication of concern for equality or fairness in payoff distribution; exploiting others’ generosity is accepted without observable hesitation or adjustment.

This player is a predominantly free-riding, payoff-maximizing type with intermittent, low-commitment cooperation. They face a binary decision each round and choose the non-contribution option far more often than the contribution option. Their behavior shows they recognize that non-contributors can systematically earn more than contributors when others keep investing. They do not display a stable contribution pattern. Instead, they alternate between stretches of consistent free-riding and occasional isolated full contributions. These cooperative moves do not appear to establish a new norm or stable phase; they are brief deviations within an otherwise self-focused strategy. Their contributions are weakly conditional on others’ behavior. They are willing to free-ride even when they observe high average contributions from the group, indicating limited concern for reciprocity or fairness in the sense of “matching” others. At the same time, the fact that they sometimes contribute when others are also contributing suggests they are not purely spiteful or purely contrarian; they accept contributing when group cooperation is already strong, but they do not maintain that stance consistently. They tolerate the risk that overall public-good payoffs might drop due to their non-contribution; they prioritize secure private payoff over maintaining a high group return. The repeated choice to free-ride in the presence of persistent cooperation by many others indicates a low weight on social-welfare considerations and a high weight on individual earnings. Overall, their “type” on the contribution dimension is best described as an opportunistic free-rider: they mostly exploit cooperative environments, occasionally contributing but not in a way that builds or stabilizes cooperation.

This player is a “selective, payoff-conscious conditional cooperator with periodic free-riding.” They do not follow a single stable contribution level; instead, their contributions fluctuate widely from full free-riding to full provision. They clearly condition on others’ behavior: when others as a group contribute relatively much, they often join in with high or full contributions, but they also occasionally exploit such cooperative environments by contributing little or nothing while others give a lot. They do not appear bound by a strict fairness norm; they tolerate contributing less than others in some instances and more than others in others, without obvious attempts to equalize. The pattern suggests a willingness to experiment with different contribution levels to probe payoffs, and a readiness to prioritize their own earnings over stable fairness. There is no clear evidence of risk aversion/ seeking beyond standard payoff responsiveness; unknown. Over time, they continue to alternate between cooperative and exploitative choices rather than converging to a consistent high- or low-contribution strategy, indicating flexible, context-sensitive behavior rather than a fixed principle.

This player is a strategic, flexible contributor who adjusts behavior to maximize payoff while maintaining some cooperative image. They are not a pure cooperator or a pure free-rider. Initial behavior suggests a moderate, “safe” contribution level rather than extremes. They later deviate sharply from the group’s stated cooperative plan to exploit others’ higher contributions, indicating willingness to defect when profitable and when personal risk is low. They subsequently make a fully cooperative contribution after others have shown strong commitment, suggesting conditional cooperation once they believe group cooperation is credible. Their pattern shows volatility: they shift between moderate, zero, and full contributions depending on expectations about others. Fairness norms appear secondary to payoff: they endorse a coordinated plan but are willing to benefit disproportionately when an opportunity arises, then later conform to high contributions when trust and norm strength seem higher. Risk tolerance appears medium: they avoid being the highest contributor early, then later take the risk of fully contributing only after clear cooperative signals from others.

This player is a systematic conditional cooperator–defector who anchors on exploiting high average group contributions but periodically “tops up” the public good. They consistently view the binary decision through a payoff lens: when others reliably contribute, they often keep their own endowment, but they intersperse full contributions at regular intervals rather than sliding into pure free-riding. This creates a patterned alternation between taking and giving rather than random volatility. Their willingness to contribute does not collapse over time; even late in the game they continue to alternate between full contribution and zero instead of moving to persistent defection. They appear less guided by strict fairness norms (e.g., “everyone should contribute every time”) and more by a hybrid of self-interest and maintaining a high group return that they can largely benefit from. Risk tolerance is moderate: they occasionally contribute even when others start deviating, but they never move to “over-contribute” relative to the rest of the group (they either match the modal norm of full giving or defect entirely). Overall, they treat contribution as one of two tools—alongside punishment—to shape payoffs, not as a moral obligation in itself.

This player is a near-unconditional high contributor and informal cooperation norm follower. They almost always contribute the full endowment and do so even when others deviate or when they personally experience punishment. The single clear deviation is a one-shot full free-ride after long, stable cooperation; they immediately revert to full contributions afterwards. That deviation looks more like a brief experiment or lapse than a strategic shift, given the immediate return to maximum giving and continued generosity despite others’ periodic under-contribution. They do not visibly condition their contributions on specific others’ behavior; they keep contributing fully even when some players give less or nothing. This suggests a strong default norm of “we should all max” and relatively low use of conditional cooperation rules like “I match the group average.” Their behavior is consistent with a player who values fast, simple, high-payoff coordination and dislikes complicating that with individualistic optimization, despite understanding that free-riding can give them a slight personal edge. They show impatience with slow play but do not translate that impatience into reduced contributions; time cost affects their mood, not their giving level. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they tolerate the risk that others may free-ride or that there may be many rounds left, while still investing fully in the public good. Fairness norms center on efficiency and equality of effort: they clearly support everyone putting in the same full amount and implicitly see deviation from that as “snaking” the group, though they do not personally react by reducing their own contributions.

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor who repeatedly chooses the maximum contribution in every observed decision, regardless of others’ deviations or short-term payoff loss. They explicitly endorse full contribution as the norm and argue for it on efficiency grounds, pointing out that low contributions marginally benefit the individual at substantial cost to the group. They do not visibly retaliate by lowering their own contribution after others free‑ride; instead they continue to cooperate and try to bring deviators back into line through communication. This suggests a strong fairness and efficiency norm, low inclination to exploit others, and relatively high tolerance for being marginally exploited if communication might fix the issue. Their risk tolerance appears moderate-to-high in social terms: they are willing to “trust” the group with continuous full contributions even after repeated defections, but they also show awareness of the payoff structure and the incentive to free‑ride.

The player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always choose the maximum contribution available and never deviate, even when others repeatedly free-ride or when it is the final round. They treat full contribution as the obvious, focal strategy and explicitly frame it as an easy way to secure high earnings and speed up the task. Their cooperation is not visibly contingent on short-term payoffs or on whether free-riders persist; they continue to contribute fully even when punishment appears not to change the main free-rider’s behavior. They show moderate impatience with delays and implicitly value efficiency: universal max contributions make the game simpler and faster, which they like. Risk tolerance appears relatively high: they commit to full cooperation even when others are not guaranteed to reciprocate, relying on group norms and punishment rather than hedging. Fairness norms are strong and equality-oriented: they expect everyone to contribute the same maximal amount, and their frustration is directed at those who deviate from this shared standard rather than trying to exploit it themselves.

This player is best described as a “quiet, payoff‑maximizing free rider with occasional conformity.” Their default is to keep the entire endowment and rely on others’ contributions to the public good. They very rarely contribute, and when they do, it coincides with full or near‑full cooperation by the rest of the group. This pattern suggests they understand that the mechanism is socially efficient (they continue to benefit from others’ giving even as total contributions fluctuate), but they personally prioritize private payoff over fairness or reciprocity. They show no evidence of conditional cooperation in the usual sense (i.e., “I’ll contribute when others do, and stop when they don’t”), because they continue not contributing even when nearly everyone else contributes consistently and the group repeatedly returns to high‑contribution states. Their few contributions look more like brief conformity to a strong norm than a stable principle: they do not maintain contributing after seeing others’ generosity and rewards, and they revert immediately to free riding. They appear insensitive to inequity in their own favor: they repeatedly accept outcomes where they earn more than high contributors, without adjusting their behavior toward more fairness. There is no sign of risk‑taking in the sense of “testing” lower group contributions; they do not try contributing in low‑contribution environments to move the group. Instead, they accept whatever contribution level others create and position themselves to gain most from it. Overall, they are a stable, low‑effort, opportunistic defector with minimal prosocial motivation.

This player is a mostly high-contribution conditional cooperator with occasional opportunistic defection. After initially free-riding once, they settle into contributing in the large majority of rounds, even when others’ average contributions are only moderate and drifting down rather than fully cooperative. This pattern suggests they value maintaining group efficiency and are willing to incur the individual cost of contributing when they see a substantial cooperative core among peers. Their cooperation is not unconditional: they sometimes withhold contribution when they see substantial free-riding by others, indicating sensitivity to perceived fairness and possible reluctance to be exploited. At the same time, they do not require unanimous or near-unanimous cooperation to continue contributing; a stable subset of reliable contributors appears sufficient for them to stay pro-social. They show moderate tolerance for partial defectors and do not immediately revert to full free-riding in response to others’ inconsistency. They appear payoff-aware rather than naïve: the environment offers a socially efficient return to contributing, and their persistent contributions amid an ongoing cooperative base imply they recognize the mutual benefit of sustaining the public good. However, because they still defect occasionally even when cooperation remains relatively strong, they also pursue self-interested gains when they deem the social cost or reputational risk acceptable. Volatility in contribution is low-to-moderate: most decisions are consistent with a “cooperate by default, defect occasionally” style rather than erratic switching. Their risk tolerance seems moderate: they are willing to keep contributing despite uncertainty about others and about the game horizon, but they hedge with some defections instead of fully committing to unconditional cooperation. Overall, they fit a “strategic cooperator” type: inclined toward building and preserving a cooperative environment, but keeping the option of self-serving deviations when they feel the group is robust enough or fairness is strained.

This player is primarily a high, almost unconditional contributor who briefly experiments with free-riding. They typically choose the cooperative option regardless of moderate variation in others’ behavior, indicating a baseline norm of “I should contribute” rather than a finely tuned conditional strategy. Their contributions are largely stable over time; the single deviation into non-contribution appears exploratory or reactive rather than a full strategy shift. They do not seem to insist on strict fairness or one-to-one reciprocity in contributions: they keep contributing even when several group members persistently contribute nothing. This suggests a tolerance for inequality and some willingness to bear exploitation, at least for a while. At the same time, the lone free-riding episode shows they are not a pure altruist; they are willing to test what happens when they do not contribute, possibly to probe whether their own cooperation is pivotal or to hedge against feeling taken advantage of. Overall, they resemble a “mostly cooperative, mildly conditional” type, but with only weak adjustment of contributions in response to others’ freeriding.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator within the available action set. Whenever a choice exists between taking all 20 or contributing all 20, the player always contributes the full amount and never deviates. This pattern persists even when others free‑ride persistently, when average returns fall, and when the game becomes unexpectedly long and frustrating. They show no strategic “testing” of defection, no retaliation, and no apparent attempt to exploit others’ contributions. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize maximizing total group payoff over short‑term individual gain, and they do not adjust downward in response to others’ selfishness. This suggests a strong internal norm of “everyone should give everything” and a willingness to personally uphold that norm regardless of others’ behavior. There is no sign of risk‑averse hedging; they repeatedly put all their endowment at risk of exploitation and tolerate being paired with multiple low contributors. They appear to treat cooperation as the default, “correct” behavior in this environment, rather than as a conditional response to how fair others are being.
