They are attentive to efficiency: very early on they explicitly reason that collectively contributing everything is the money‑maximizing strategy. They stick to that efficiency rule for the rest of the game, even when the marginal returns in practice are reduced by others’ partial or non‑contribution. They do not diversify across strategies or try to fine‑tune contributions; they pick “always contribute all” and commit to it for the duration, despite the game going on longer than advertised and despite clear evidence of exploitable defectors.

This player is a strongly cooperative, near-maximum contributor. They consistently give their full endowment except for a single instance where they reduce their contribution modestly while the group’s average also drops. There is no evidence they ever free‑ride or react punitively to others’ low contributions by cutting back substantially. Their behavior suggests they adopt “always cooperate” as a default norm and maintain it even when some others deviate. Their one non‑max choice appears situational rather than the start of a downward trend: they immediately return to full contributions afterward and stay there through the end. This points to high contribution stability and low volatility overall. They do not explicitly discuss contribution strategy in detail, but their early affirmation of going “max” aligns with the emerging group norm and they stay consistent with that norm. They appear willing to accept the risk that others might undercontribute, as they maintain high contributions even when others evidently are not all doing maximum. This indicates relatively high risk tolerance in service of group efficiency. Their fairness norm seems to emphasize “everyone should contribute maximum” rather than tit‑for‑tat adjustments; they do not condition their own contribution on specific others’ behaviors.

This player is a payoff-focused, opportunistic conditional cooperator with substantial free-riding. Contributions span the entire range, from full free-riding to giving the full endowment, and vary frequently rather than stabilizing on a fixed rule. The player clearly understands that contributing can be profitable when others are contributing a lot: they are willing to make high or even maximal contributions in environments where cooperation is strong. At the same time, they repeatedly take advantage of others’ high contributions by contributing nothing and keeping their full endowment, suggesting limited internalization of fairness norms and a comfort with exploiting cooperative surroundings. Their contribution choices do not appear random: they cluster around a pattern of “sometimes join the high contributors, sometimes free-ride on them,” with intermediate contributions used as well. There is no evidence that they systematically match others’ exact average behavior, but they do seem responsive to the general cooperative climate: they are more willing to contribute when many others are giving a lot and still generating high returns, and they free-ride more in periods where they can do so without collapsing contributions altogether. There is no sign of altruistic sacrifice beyond what is individually profitable; when they do contribute heavily, it appears compatible with self-interest under the existing high group contribution level. Overall, this type uses contribution levels instrumentally rather than normatively. They tolerate inequity in their own favor and do not consistently sacrifice payoff to uphold equal effort or fairness. Risk tolerance regarding others’ future cooperation is moderate: they repeatedly free-ride without immediately testing whether this will crash group contributions, implying either a belief that others will keep cooperating or a willingness to ride out volatility while maintaining their own high payoffs.

This player is a highly stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund and never deviate, even when doing so is clearly unprofitable relative to others’ lower contributions. Their behavior suggests they treat full contribution as a rule or norm rather than as a calculation-sensitive choice. They display strong commitment to group efficiency and fairness norms: they persist in contributing fully when others also do so, and they continue to fully contribute even when one peer intermittently free rides. They do not appear to condition their own contribution on others’ past behavior; their cooperation is insensitive to both good and bad behavior from partners. This points to a “principled cooperator” or “norm-following altruist” type rather than a conditional cooperator. There is no evidence of risk aversion about being exploited in contribution decisions. They consistently accept the risk and reality of exploitation by a lower contributor, maintaining full cooperation. They also show no apparent attempt to hedge or test others with partial or zero contributions, even after repeated deviations by a peer, which indicates low focus on payoff-maximizing adaptation in the contribution stage. Because they cannot see how many rounds remain, their unwavering full contributions also indicate that they do not attempt any endgame defection or strategic tapering. They behave as if the appropriate action is to contribute fully every time, regardless of horizon. Overall, their contribution “type” is: rigid, norm-driven full cooperator, non-conditional on others’ recent behavior, willing to absorb personal disadvantage to maintain cooperative norms.

This player is a moderately prosocial, payoff-sensitive conditional cooperator with conservative risk-taking and no strong fairness enforcement. They rarely choose extreme contribution levels. Their typical choice lies in the lower-to-middle range of the action space, with only a few excursions to very low or very high amounts. This indicates a general tendency to contribute something to the group rather than fully free-riding, but also a reluctance to sacrifice too much private payoff. Their behavior is somewhat volatile early on, with noticeable shifts between low, medium, and very high contributions. Over time, contributions stabilize into a narrower band around a modest, repeatable level. This pattern suggests ongoing learning and calibration rather than a fixed rule from the outset. They clearly condition their behavior on the observed cooperativeness of the group. When others appear to contribute more, they are willing to increase their own contributions. When the environment looks less cooperative or when high contributions do not translate into especially high payoffs, they pull back. However, their adjustments are incremental rather than drastic, which points to a cautious and measured response style. They do not appear to follow a strict fairness or reciprocity rule (such as matching the group average or strongly punishing low contributors by sharply cutting their own contributions). Instead, they seem to balance “doing their part” with maintaining a comfortable individual payoff. This balance implies a weak fairness norm: they care about contributing above pure self-interest, but they do not push for equal burden-sharing or maximal group efficiency at personal cost. Their risk tolerance is moderate. They are willing to occasionally make high contributions, which are risky in this environment given the incentives to free-ride and the absence of punishment or reward tools. However, these high contributions are rare and followed by more self-protective behavior. They seem to experiment with generosity, observe the outcome, and then retreat toward safer, mid-range contributions when the payoff or group response does not justify sustained high giving. Overall, this player is best characterized as a cautious conditional cooperator who aims for a compromise between benefiting the group and safeguarding personal earnings, updating their contributions based on the emerging cooperative climate but without strong moralistic or enforcement-driven motives.

This player is a highly stable, near-pure free-rider who treats contributing as almost never worthwhile. Across the game they repeatedly choose the lowest feasible contribution, only deviating from zero once and only minimally. Their pattern does not track group generosity: they keep their own contribution essentially fixed while others’ average behavior and total contributions fluctuate substantially. This indicates very low conditional cooperation; they do not seem to reward high group contributions with higher own contributions, nor do they retaliate against declines by further reducing their already minimal giving. They appear payoff-maximizing in a narrow, self-regarding sense, matching the dominant-strategy logic of the public goods game: they routinely keep their entire endowment and take the return from others’ contributions. The single small deviation from zero is too minor and isolated to confidently interpret as a principled shift (it could reflect curiosity, misclick, or a brief test), so their core type is best described as consistently non-contributory. They display high tolerance for exploiting others’ cooperation: they free-ride even when others contribute generously and continue to do so despite sustained evidence that many peers are willing to put substantial amounts into the public pool. There is no sign that they aim at fairness or proportionality between their own giving and others’; they do not “match” the group or try to equalize benefits and burdens. Their behavior suggests either a belief that personal contributions are strategically dominated, or a simple preference for maximizing private payoff regardless of group norms. Risk tolerance around contribution is low: they do not experiment with varying contribution levels, do not hedge with intermediate contributions, and do not probe whether different behavior might change group dynamics. Instead, they “lock in” an almost invariant strategy early and stick to it. There is no visible learning toward greater cooperation over time; if anything, the stable low contributions from start to finish suggest they enter with, and maintain, a free-rider plan rather than adapting to the emergent cooperative environment. In new PGG environments, this type is best modeled as a steadfast low contributor who: (a) defaults to zero, (b) is largely insensitive to others’ contributions, (c) does not attempt to enforce fairness through own giving, and (d) aims to extract the benefits of others’ cooperation while preserving their full endowment. Their rare, tiny deviation from zero should be treated as noise or low-confidence exploration, not as evidence of underlying conditional cooperation.

This player is a stable, moderate contributor who neither free-rides nor fully cooperates. They consistently choose a mid-level contribution that splits resources between private payoff and the public fund. Their behavior is stable rather than volatile: they do not adjust their contribution when others’ behavior shifts markedly between rounds. This suggests either limited responsiveness to social signals or a simple, pre-committed rule (e.g., “split roughly half / play safe”). They do not appear to condition contributions strongly on others’ cooperativeness, despite observing clear variation, including both very high and very low contributors. This points to weak conditional cooperation and a focus on steady, individually satisfactory outcomes rather than strict fairness enforcement. Their contributions are compatible with a moderate fairness norm (they are not exploiting others), but they show no evidence of trying to match the highest contributors or punish the lowest via their own contribution choices. Risk tolerance appears balanced: they avoid the extremes of fully defecting or fully contributing, which is consistent with a cautious, satisficing strategy under uncertainty about others.

The player is a mostly high contributor with intermittent strategic free-riding and an initial learning phase. Early in the game, they misunderstand the interface and alternate between taking all and giving all, while explicitly stating confusion. Once they understand how to contribute, they reliably give the full endowment in most rounds, aligning with the group’s cooperation norm. However, even after learning, they occasionally revert to taking everything while others mostly contribute; these deviations are not announced or justified and coincide with them earning clearly higher payoffs than unconditional cooperators. This pattern suggests a conditional cooperator who is willing to exploit opportunities for quiet free-riding once norms are established and social pressure is focused on someone else (e.g., a persistent low contributor). They do not threaten or coordinate sanctions; instead, they “opt out” of full cooperation for a few rounds while verbally supporting a tolerant, “it’s just a game” stance. Their risk tolerance regarding social disapproval appears low: they do not challenge norm violators directly and rarely stand out from the group’s stated preferences, but they accept some moral hazard by occasionally defecting when attention is elsewhere. Fairness norms are mixed: they publicly support letting others play as they wish and frame the situation as low-stakes, yet they quietly benefit from others’ high contributions without compensating or signaling remorse beyond earlier apologies tied strictly to misunderstanding, not to later deliberate free-riding.

This player fits a “quiet, mildly opportunistic cooperator” type. They consistently keep contributions below the emerging high-cooperation norm, even when others are often near-maximal contributors. Their contributions vary substantially across rounds, with both very low and high amounts, indicating volatility rather than a fixed rule. The pattern suggests conditional behavior primarily based on short-term self-payoff considerations rather than strict fairness or norm adherence. They do not appear to try to match the group’s prevailing high contributions; instead, they often under-contribute relative to most peers, free-riding on others’ generosity while still benefiting from the multiplied public fund. The presence of some high contributions indicates they are not a pure defector and are willing to contribute significantly at times, but there is no clear, simple tit-for-tat or matching rule visible—changes in their contributions do not clearly map onto identifiable shifts in group behavior. Fairness norms are weak or unknown; they do not seem driven to align with group averages or to punish low contributors via reduced giving (since their own giving is often below the group anyway). They show willingness to contribute fully only once at the very end, which may reflect either an end-game gesture toward cooperation, confusion about optimal play, or simple experimentation; the motive is unknown. Overall, they tolerate risk in the sense that they ride on others’ cooperation without attempting to stabilize norms through communication or rewards, accepting the possibility that cooperation could unravel.

This player is a steadfast high contributor who consistently gives the maximum allowed amount every round. Their behavior is extremely stable rather than exploratory or reactive; they do not adjust their contribution in response to others’ free-riding or partial contributions, nor when others openly experiment with lower amounts. They appear to adopt a simple “full cooperation” rule and stick with it throughout, even when it is clear that some others are contributing little or nothing. They express a belief that contributing all “seems the most logical,” indicating that they frame the game as one where full cooperation is the rational or normatively correct choice, likely because they intuit the group-level efficiency of contributions being multiplied and shared. They speculate that low contributors may either have constraints or be “greedy,” showing awareness of fairness norms and a mild moral evaluation of free-riding, but this does not translate into reduced giving. They tolerate substantial social risk of exploitation and do not appear to hedge against free-riders, suggesting either strong pro-social preferences, a focus on the experiment’s cooperative ideal, or low concern for payoff maximization relative to maintaining a cooperative stance.

This player is a generally cooperative, above-average contributor who becomes increasingly generous over time, with some early experimentation and mild conditionality. They begin with a modest, below-group contribution, then quickly move into mid-to-high contributions, and ultimately adopt a near “full-cooperator” stance for most later rounds. They are not rigid: they adjust contributions both upward and downward in response to the evolving group pattern rather than following a fixed rule. They show some mild opportunism or caution: after the group experiences a notable wave of very low contributions, they briefly reduce their own amount from their recent maximum, despite ongoing chat pressure to “all give the maximum.” This suggests they understand the incentive to avoid being exploited and are willing to hedge when trust is uncertain. However, that hedging is limited in scope and duration, and they soon return to fully cooperative contributions once the group re-coordinates on high giving. Overall, their contribution strategy is “conditional cooperator leaning strongly toward high cooperation”: they are willing to carry their share or more when they see enough others doing likewise, but they do react to visible free-riding by adjusting downward slightly. They appear comfortable taking on some risk of being exploited (they often give the maximum even when some players continue to contribute very little), indicating moderate-to-high risk tolerance in the social domain and a preference for efficient group outcomes over strict payoff-maximizing self-interest.

This player is a mixed, learning-oriented cooperator who gradually gravitates to full cooperation once they trust the group. Early on, they sometimes keep their endowment while verbally aligning with the “all in” norm, indicating confusion and/or opportunistic hesitation. They appear to misunderstand the interface at points and believe they are contributing when the record shows they are not, then explicitly acknowledge that they “think they’ve got it now” once the misunderstanding is clarified. After that learning phase, they mostly match the cooperative consensus and are willing to contribute their full endowment when they expect others to do the same. Their pattern is not mechanically stable—there are occasional zero contributions even after they understand the game—but these look more like short-lived deviations than a sustained free-riding strategy. They accept the group’s fairness norm that contributing is “for all of us” and are responsive to social pressure and advice from others. Overall, they are conditionally cooperative: they want the group outcome to be high, tend to go along with cooperative norms, but will sometimes defect quietly when tempted or uncertain.

This player is a moderate, flexible cooperator who oscillates between below-average and above-average contributions rather than locking into a single level. They do not follow a simple linear pattern (e.g., monotonic decay); instead, they adjust in a context-sensitive but somewhat noisy way. They at times contribute less than most others, and at other times match or exceed the group’s central tendency, including making at least one fully cooperative move. This suggests they care about cooperation but are not purely altruistic or purely self-interested. Their behavior is conditionally cooperative in a loose sense: they appear responsive to group contribution levels, but not with precise proportionality. They neither clearly “punish” low group contributions by sharply dropping their own, nor blindly reward high group contributions by always increasing. Instead, they sample different contribution levels, sometimes moving against the group trend. This implies exploratory behavior and moderate risk tolerance: they are willing to try both low and high contributions to probe payoffs and social responses. They show no strict fairness norm (such as always matching the group average or leading by a fixed margin). However, they often stay within a middle range relative to others rather than extreme free-riding or unwavering maximal giving. This points to a “balanced opportunist” type: they value earnings and are sensitive to social context but still accept some personal cost to keep group provision non-trivially high.

This player is a strong, near-unconditional high contributor who adopts “everyone maxes out” as the focal norm. They consistently give the full endowment whenever possible, with a single brief deviation to free ride once and then an immediate return to maximum contributions. That lone defection does not become a pattern, suggesting an experiment or slip rather than a strategic shift. They appear to accept that full contribution is individually profitable under the high multiplier and also socially fair. They do not require explicit reward or punishment to maintain cooperation as long as the group is cooperating. Even when one other player temporarily deviates to zero, they themselves keep contributing the maximum, indicating low inclination to retaliate via reduced contributions and a preference for preserving the cooperative equilibrium.

This player is a consistently high contributor with mild downward adjustment. They repeatedly allocate a large majority of their endowment to the public fund, usually in the upper mid-to-high range rather than at the extremes. Their contributions vary within a narrow band and never drop to low or zero, indicating stable pro-social intent rather than opportunistic free-riding. They do not rigidly match the exact group average, but their contribution is generally above or close to it, suggesting a cooperative baseline with some awareness of efficiency and group behavior. When overall group contributions fluctuate, they adjust slightly rather than dramatically, which points to moderate conditional cooperation: they care about the group level of giving, but they do not punish lower cooperation by sharply cutting their own contributions. They do not chase the individually payoff-maximizing strategy of minimal contribution, nor do they consistently maximize contribution to the public fund. This hints at a balance between a social preference for efficiency/equity and a self-interested concern for retaining some private payoff. They appear relatively risk-tolerant in the sense of repeatedly investing in the public fund even when there is visible variation in other players’ behavior, but they are not so risk-tolerant as to go all-in. Their pattern suggests a cooperative, efficiency-oriented type who values sustaining a high public good while preserving some personal gain, and who is resistant to short-term temptation to defect even when others do so. There is no clear sign of strong concern for strict equality or for punishing unfairness through their own contributions; instead, they prioritize maintaining a robust but not maximal cooperative level. Overall, they fit a “steady, moderately conditional cooperator” profile.

This player is a persistent low contributor who opportunistically free-rides on others’ cooperation. After a brief and limited early exploration of contributing, they quickly settle into near-constant zero contributions despite group contributions staying high. Their overall pattern is strongly self-regarding: they reliably keep their full endowment whenever possible and only deviate from that in a few isolated instances. Their contribution path is volatile at the very beginning and then stabilizes: they try several different low-to-moderate amounts and then switch to systematic non-contribution. This suggests early experimentation with the environment, followed by a clear strategic commitment to exploitative free-riding once they see that others continue to contribute generously. They are not conditionally cooperative in the usual sense. Even when the group’s average contribution is high and remains high over time, they do not reciprocate; instead they reduce and then eliminate their own contributions. They treat others’ cooperation as a resource to be exploited rather than a norm to match. They do not appear to follow fairness norms regarding proportionality between what one gives and what one receives. They repeatedly accept payoffs that are higher than those of many heavy contributors without adjusting upward. This indicates that inequality in their favor is acceptable to them, and they show no behavioral sign of guilt, fairness correction, or desire to “do their share” once it is clear others are carrying the public good. Their risk tolerance appears low regarding payoff sacrifice. The early small and moderate contributions look like limited tests rather than altruistic commitments. Once they infer that contributing is not necessary for earning high payoffs—because others are reliably cooperative—they stop risking any of their endowment. They do not gamble on increasing the public fund further with their own coins; they prefer the sure personal gain of keeping everything, trusting that others’ cooperative tendencies will maintain a good group return. Overall, their contribution “type” is best described as an informed, stable free-rider: they gather enough early information to understand how the game works, then commit to keeping their endowment and living off others’ public contributions.

This player is a moderately high but not extreme contributor, with contributions usually in the middle-to-upper range of the allowed spectrum rather than at either full defection or full cooperation. Their behavior shows a willingness to give substantially to the public fund while clearly preserving some private payoff. Contribution levels are somewhat variable but stay within a relatively narrow band; there is no drift toward pure free-riding, nor a stable pattern of maximal giving. This suggests they do not follow a rigid rule (e.g., always give the same amount) but also avoid drastic swings. Changes in their contributions appear loosely responsive to the general environment rather than random: they sometimes increase after periods in which others appear broadly cooperative, and they sometimes reduce their own contributions after stretches where the payoff structure makes it apparent that free-riders and low contributors earn more than cooperators. However, they never move into consistently minimal contributions, indicating a persistent cooperative inclination. They seem to understand the social efficiency of contributing: their average giving is above what a purely self-interested, payoff-maximizing free-rider would choose in this setting. At the same time, they do not sacrifice as much as the most generous players, indicating a balance between self-interest and group interest rather than pure altruism. This points to a conditional cooperator with a built-in floor of cooperation: they are willing to keep contributing even when incentives favor more selfish play, but they also seek to maintain relative payoff competitiveness. Their fairness norm appears to be “contribute a reasonable, shared load, but not more than those who are clearly giving a lot.” They are neither the lowest nor the highest contributor in the group, positioning themselves around the cooperative middle. When others in aggregate are still contributing substantially, they maintain or raise their own contribution rather than exploiting that generosity to the maximum. Yet they occasionally pull back from higher contributions after episodes where low contributors and non-contributors visibly obtain higher individual payoffs, showing sensitivity to perceived exploitation and payoff inequity. Risk tolerance looks moderate. They accept some risk of earning less than short-term free-riders by contributing above the minimum, but they do not take the extreme “high-risk altruist” stance of always giving close to the maximum. They appear willing to test slightly higher contributions, then adjust downward when doing so coincides with them being relatively worse off than more selfish players. This suggests they attend closely to realized payoffs and are willing to trade off some efficiency for personal security when inequities become salient. Overall, their strategy resembles a learning, payoff-aware conditional cooperator: start and remain cooperative, modulate contributions in response to the evolving mix of high and low contributors, but keep a baseline of prosocial giving even without direct enforcement mechanisms.

This player is a high, near-unconditional cooperator whose baseline is to give the full endowment to the public fund. They contribute the maximum in almost all rounds, even when others free‑ride or when they themselves are targeted with deductions. Deviations from full contribution are rare and situational rather than a sustained strategy; they briefly keep their endowment on a couple of occasions, which appears more like experimentation or opportunism than a wholesale shift in norms. They accept that the public good is strongly profitable and behave as if the “social optimum” (everyone giving full) is both normatively right and practically best. Their willingness to keep contributing fully even in the presence of a persistent free rider suggests relatively low risk aversion to being exploited and a strong internal fairness norm favoring contribution, tempered by occasional self-interested or playful deviations. They are sensitive to others’ cooperative behavior (praising or celebrating when a defector finally contributes) but do not condition their own contributions tightly on others; instead they rely on punishment and reward to manage deviations while keeping their own giving high.

This player is a moderately cooperative, payoff-sensitive contributor who settles on a stable, partial-contribution strategy. They do not adopt the fully cooperative norm that emerges in group discussion, instead choosing a middle level of giving that remains constant across the game. Their behavior suggests they prioritize securing a high personal return while still participating in the public good enough to benefit from others’ higher contributions. They show no visible conditional response to others’ individual deviations; their contribution level stays the same even as group behavior becomes more heterogeneous and average contributions drop. This indicates low sensitivity to observed free-riding or generosity at the individual level, and a preference for a simple, pre-set rule over fine-tuned conditional cooperation. Their choices are consistent with a self-interested but not purely selfish calculus: they do not exploit as hard as possible, but they clearly do not aim to match or uphold the most cooperative standard either. Overall, they appear to follow a “safe middle” heuristic: contribute enough to appear cooperative and capture public-good benefits, but hold back enough to maintain strong private payoffs regardless of what others do.

This player is a highly volatile, payoff‑sensitive conditional cooperator with opportunistic free‑riding streaks. They clearly understand that contributing can be profitable when others are generous: they are willing to choose the full endowment or other high amounts on multiple occasions, even after low personal payoffs, which suggests they believe group cooperation is attainable and materially beneficial. At the same time, they repeatedly shift to very low or zero contributions after observing that they can earn more by riding on others’ generosity. Their contribution levels span the entire range from zero to the full endowment, with no simple monotonic trend; instead, behavior oscillates in response to recent experience. They do not follow a rigid “always cooperate” or “always defect” rule. Instead, they appear to explore and re‑optimize: when group contributions seem strong or rebounding, they are willing to match or exceed the emerging cooperative norm with mid‑to‑high contributions. When the environment looks exploitable or less predictable, they quickly pull back to low amounts to protect their own payoff. They also demonstrate willingness to “re‑invest” in cooperation even after being exploited, indicating they are not purely spiteful; their main concern seems to be expected monetary return rather than enforcing fairness for its own sake. Fairness considerations are present but secondary. They show tolerance for disadvantageous inequality when it pays: they repeatedly accept others’ higher payoffs while free‑riding. Conversely, they also accept being among the lower earners when they choose to cooperate fully in relatively uncooperative environments. This flexibility suggests a pragmatic, outcome‑focused rather than principle‑driven fairness norm: they care about efficiency and their own gains more than strict equality, and they do not rigidly target matching others’ average contributions. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high. They frequently stake the full or near‑full endowment despite no punishment or reward instruments, implying comfort with exposure to the risk of unilateral cooperation. However, they repeatedly offset this with later low or zero contributions, indicating they actively manage risk across time by alternating between high‑exposure cooperative moves and safe free‑riding phases. Their contribution rule can be summarized as: “Cooperate strongly when others seem willing and the marginal returns look high, but periodically and opportunistically defect to capture extra surplus, and pull back sharply when the public good looks weak or unstable.”

This player is a highly cooperative, near-unconditional high contributor who anchors on the group norm of full contribution and very rarely deviates. They consistently put in the maximum amount almost every round, including early on when the group norm is uncertain and later when a few others temporarily reduce their contributions. This pattern suggests they prioritize collective efficiency and shared payoff over short-term individual gain. Their behavior indicates a strong preference for stable, symmetric cooperation: they explicitly endorse the norm that everyone should contribute fully and then personally adhere to it, even without enforcement mechanisms. They do not appear to condition their own contributions on minor free-riding by others; brief small deviations from the group norm by some players do not trigger a lasting reduction in their own giving. The very few non-max contributions suggest mild experimentation or fatigue rather than a principled shift toward defection; they immediately revert to full contribution once the group resumes full cooperation. They show an understanding that full contributions from all players maximize total group earnings, and they willingly align their behavior with that understanding. Their readiness to contribute fully from the outset, and to keep doing so across the game, reflects low risk aversion regarding potential exploitation: they are willing to risk being taken advantage of to sustain a cooperative equilibrium. Fairness for them appears to mean “everyone does their part at the high-cooperation level,” and they act in line with that standard rather than withholding contributions in response to small deviations. Overall, their contribution “type” is best described as a norm-committed, group-efficiency-oriented cooperator with very high contribution stability, very low reactivity to small-scale free-riding, and a preference for maintaining a cooperative group equilibrium even under uncertainty about game length and others’ long-term intentions.

This player is a steadfast full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum possible amount and never deviate, even when others free‑ride or when contributions in the group drop. Their strategy is stable rather than reactive: they do not reduce their own giving in response to others’ low contributions or to persistent noncompliance by several group members. They appear to follow a simple fairness norm: everyone should contribute the maximum, and this is seen as both morally and materially correct. From their behavior and comments, they understand that full contribution by all yields the highest joint payoff, and they act consistently with that belief, showing low concern for short‑term personal gain at the expense of the group. There is no evidence of risk‑averse hedging or conditional cooperation; they do not “test” others by lowering their own contribution when some players underprovide. Their willingness to keep giving fully despite repeated defection by some group members suggests a strong intrinsic or norm-based commitment to cooperation.

This player is a conditional, payoff-sensitive contributor who mixes cooperation and free-riding rather than committing to a single mode. They are clearly willing to contribute fully to the public good at many points, but they also repeatedly choose to contribute nothing even when others continue to contribute. Their pattern shows that they do not follow a simple “always cooperate” norm; instead, they appear to compare the private payoff from free-riding against the shared payoff from joint contribution and are willing to exploit cooperative others when it is lucrative. Their contribution behavior is moderately volatile: they switch several times between full contribution and zero contribution across the game instead of settling quickly into a single stable choice. However, they are not random: stretches of sustained full contribution alternate with stretches of sustained free-riding, suggesting they update based on perceived trends in the group or on recent payoff experiences rather than round-by-round emotional reactions. They do not enforce strict fairness norms. They continue to contribute in the presence of at least one persistent free-rider and do not systematically withdraw cooperation in direct proportion to others’ selfishness. At the same time, they also free-ride even when multiple others are cooperating, indicating that “if others are fair, I must also be fair” is not a binding rule for them. Instead, their fairness concern is either weak or consistently overridden by payoff considerations. Risk tolerance appears moderate. They are willing to be the only or one of very few contributors in some rounds, exposing themselves to the risk of low redistribution when others do not join. Yet they also protect themselves at times by switching to free-riding, especially after experiencing scenarios in which contributing yields relatively poor private payoffs compared to free-riding under similar group behavior. This pattern suggests a player who recognizes the strategic risk of unilateral cooperation and is willing to take it sometimes, but not in a rigid or idealistic way. Overall, their contribution “type” is a flexible, opportunistic cooperator: inclined to cooperate when it seems worthwhile or when others are joining in, but ready to revert to self-interested free-riding to secure high individual payoffs and to avoid bearing the cost of sustaining cooperation alone.

This player is a strict, unconditional free rider. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute, regardless of how others behave. Their behavior is fully stable from start to finish; there is no experimentation, no switch after seeing very high or very low group contributions, and no apparent sensitivity to which specific peers are contributing or not. They accept large benefits from others’ cooperation without ever matching it, indicating that fairness or equity concerns do not constrain their decisions in this environment. They also do not appear to use contributions as a signal or coordination device; they consistently treat the game as purely individual payoff maximization, ignoring the public good dimension. Risk tolerance about others’ future cooperation is effectively irrelevant for them, because they never put any of their own resources at stake. Overall, the dominant motive appears to be maximizing own coins each round in a myopic, stage-game–optimal way, with no sign of conditional cooperation, reciprocity, or sacrifice for efficiency.

This player is a consistently high contributor who treats strong cooperation as the default. They typically contribute well above the group average and rarely drop to clearly self-serving levels. Their giving pattern suggests an internal norm of “do my fair share or more,” rather than a strict optimization of private payoff. They adjust contributions modestly in response to others’ behavior: when the group cooperates strongly, they sustain or increase their own contribution; when some group members reduce contributions or free-ride, they scale back slightly but do not retaliate harshly or switch to full defection. This indicates conditional cooperation tempered by a strong pro-social baseline. They appear willing to bear personal cost to support a high-contribution environment, even when others are not fully matching them. Risk-wise, they accept the possibility of being exploited by low contributors, suggesting either a preference for the cooperative outcome or a belief that visible generosity can encourage others to improve. Their fairness norm seems group-focused and inclusive: they support joint gains even when some players lag, rather than tightly matching the lowest contributors.

The player is a stable, uncompromising high contributor who consistently gives the full endowment to the public good. Contribution behavior is non-conditional on others’ cooperation: they continue to fully cooperate even when average group contributions decline or oscillate. This indicates a strong internalized norm for “do the socially efficient thing” rather than a reactive “match others” rule. They appear willing to tolerate exploitation risk and do not reduce contributions as a response to free-riding, suggesting either (a) a principled commitment to full cooperation, (b) a long-run expectation that high contributions will be rewarded or reciprocated, or (c) low sensitivity to short-term payoff optimization. Fairness seems interpreted more as “I should do my part (and more if needed)” than “I should match the group average.” There is no evidence of strategic contribution fluctuation or endgame defection; they maintain the same pattern through to the end, implying either unawareness of the game horizon, indifference to it, or a choice to ignore it in favor of a fixed cooperative rule.

This player is an initially cooperative but quickly conditional contributor who shifts to free-riding after experiencing targeted punishment. They begin by fully investing in the public good when others are mostly cooperative, indicating a baseline willingness to act pro-socially even when doing so is personally costly. They later switch to non-contribution despite others continuing to contribute at high rates, suggesting that their cooperation is fragile and strongly contingent on how they are treated rather than on group norms alone. The switch away from contributing follows being punished once, indicating that they interpret punishment as a negative social signal or injustice rather than as a norm-enforcing cue, and they respond by withdrawing cooperation. Their choices do not aim to fine-tune contributions based on subtle gradations of others’ behavior; instead, they display a coarse, state-like pattern (all-in cooperation vs full defection). This pattern points to a player who values fairness and respect; when they feel unfairly targeted, they are willing to accept substantial efficiency losses to stop contributing. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they initially “risk” cooperating in a mixed environment but do not persist in cooperative behavior after a salient negative interaction.

This player is a strict, unwavering full contributor. They always contribute the entire endowment and never deviate, even when a group member slightly under-contributes once. Their behavior suggests they treat full contribution as the default, not as a conditional response to others’ exact choices. They follow a stable, rule-like strategy rather than experimenting or adjusting. There is no exploration of alternative contribution levels, even after seeing that contributing less is possible and occasionally chosen by someone else. This indicates low behavioral volatility and a strong internal norm or principle in favor of full cooperation. They appear to operate under a strong efficiency and group-welfare logic: given a marginal per-capita return above 1 for the group as a whole, they behave as if the “correct” action is to maximize the public good, and they act accordingly. They are not observed to hedge against possible future defection, so their risk tolerance with respect to others free-riding is unknown, but there is no evidence of strategic caution; they do not scale back contributions when someone briefly deviates. Their fairness norm seems tied to symmetry and mutual full cooperation: they contribute at the maximum level and accept that everyone (including themselves) benefits equally from the pool, regardless of any slight, short-lived deviations by others. The brief under-contribution by another player does not lead them to express dissatisfaction via lower contributions. This suggests they prioritize maintaining full-cooperation norms over finely matching others’ behavior. There is no evidence of end-game decay or attempts to exploit others near the end; they continue to fully cooperate all the way through. This supports the view that they are guided more by a stable contribution rule or norm than by backward-looking strategic calculation about remaining rounds. Learning or adaptation in contribution levels is essentially absent: once they adopt full contribution, they keep it. In a new PGG environment, this player is best characterized as a “principled full cooperator”: they strongly prefer contributing the maximum, are highly stable over time, and do not condition their own contribution on minor deviations in others’ behavior as long as group cooperation remains high.

This player is a stable full cooperator. They always contribute the maximum available amount and never experiment with free-riding, even after repeated exposure to others contributing nothing and even when reward patterns and chat make clear that some players profit more by withholding. Their cooperation is not visibly contingent on short-term individual payoff; instead, it aligns with a group-efficiency norm and a desire to return to “high group earning” situations. They explicitly reference the higher payoffs when everyone cooperates, indicating that they understand the efficiency benefits and care about them. There is no evidence of risk-averse hedging or retaliatory defection; they tolerate others’ partial or zero contributions without reducing their own, suggesting a principled or norm-based cooperator type rather than a conditional cooperator.

This player is a generally high, norm-following contributor who occasionally experiments with defection. Across the game they choose the cooperative option far more often than the selfish one, so their baseline type is “conditional cooperator with principled leanings.” They appear to adopt a simple rule: contribute fully when a substantial core of others also does so, and withhold only sporadically when cooperation levels are low or mixed. Their contributions are mostly stable and not strongly volatile. Isolated deviations from full contribution do not cascade into a persistent free-riding strategy; after trying non-contribution, they quickly return to full cooperation even if the group does not uniformly improve. This suggests they value maintaining a cooperative standard more than strictly optimizing against short-run incentives. They display a concern for fairness: they keep contributing even when several others free-ride, and they combine this with selective punishment (discussed below). They seem willing to bear personal cost to support a group norm of high contribution, which is consistent with an internalized fairness or reciprocity norm rather than pure payoff maximization. Their tolerance for risk is moderate. They continue to contribute fully in an environment where some players regularly give nothing, indicating they are prepared to “risk” being exploited for the possibility of sustaining cooperation. However, the occasional zero contribution shows they are not blindly altruistic; they are willing to test what happens when they themselves free-ride, likely to gauge both income effects and social feedback via punishment. Conditioning on others’ behavior is present but not extreme. When the environment looks strongly cooperative, they reliably cooperate; when it is mixed or deteriorating, they still usually contribute but occasionally defect. They do not engage in rapid tit-for-tat or fine-grained matching; their reactions are coarse and slow, consistent with a player who tracks the general cooperative climate rather than individual histories (which they cannot fully observe here).

This player is a stable, unconditional high contributor. They always contribute their full endowment in every round, even when others free-ride heavily, when coordination attempts to “refuse to donate” emerge, and when the game reaches the final round. Their strategy does not appear to adjust to changes in group behavior, observed defection, or horizon effects. This suggests either a fixed norm of “always contribute” or a deliberate choice to prioritize group efficiency over individual earnings, regardless of others’ actions. They tolerate substantial exploitation by free-riders without retaliating or downgrading their own contributions. Their behavior is consistent with a strong cooperative or rule-following type rather than a conditional cooperator or payoff-maximizer.

The player fits a “quiet, profit‑maximizing free rider with occasional conformity” type. Their baseline is to keep almost all of their endowment and contribute minimally, even when others contribute fully and explicitly advocate universal giving. They do not match the prevailing norm of full contribution and never sustain high contributions across time. Their contributions fluctuate between very low and moderate levels rather than converging on a stable pattern, suggesting opportunistic experimentation rather than a principled norm. They clearly prioritize individual payoff over group efficiency: they repeatedly choose contribution levels that maximize their private returns in the given environment, even when a stable, high‑contribution norm is visible and reinforced in chat. Occasional higher contributions appear sporadic and are not tied to any expressed reasoning or explicit coordination, so their underlying decision rule is unknown; however, those higher contributions do not persist when they see that others continue contributing high amounts regardless of their own behavior. They show no visible fairness norm: they regularly contribute far less than the group while benefiting from others’ generosity, without any evident attempt to “make up for it” later or to track proportionality. Their risk tolerance regarding potential social sanctions is high; they free ride openly in the presence of identifiable partners and critical chat but do not adjust towards more cooperative behavior in response to social pressure alone.

This player is a verbally pro-cooperation “team player” who nonetheless opportunistically free-rides when it is safe and profitable. They initially match a moderately high cooperative norm and then quickly endorse the group’s push toward full contribution. Their stated intentions track the cooperative equilibrium implied by the multiplier, and they explicitly commit to increasing their contribution alongside others. However, their actual pattern is conditional and self-serving rather than principled: after having experienced full mutual cooperation and seeing that the group can coordinate, they later reduce their own contribution while continuing to present themselves as aligned with the high-contribution plan. This indicates a willingness to exploit trust once a cooperative norm is in place, suggesting a strategic, payoff-sensitive cooperative stance rather than unconditional altruism. They tolerate inequality in their favor and show no attempt to correct it through higher giving or punishment, consistent with an egoistic payoff focus under a cooperative façade. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to cooperate early when it is unclear how others will behave, but later they reduce their contribution only after the norm is established, which lowers the risk of coordinated punishment or social backlash.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no variation and no apparent sensitivity to others’ behavior. They do not reduce contributions in response to clearly visible free-riding, erratic behavior, or heavy use of punishment and reward by others. This suggests a strong internal norm or commitment to maximizing the group public good, independent of short-term personal payoff optimization. They appear risk-tolerant with respect to exploitation: they continue fully cooperating even when some others repeatedly contribute nothing or very little. There is no sign that they condition contributions on fairness, reciprocity, or average group contribution levels; their strategy is simple and consistent rather than contingent. Their type is best characterized as a norm-driven unconditional cooperator rather than a calculating conditional cooperator.

This player is a steady, high contributor after a brief early experiment with free-riding. They alternate between medium and zero giving at the very start, then quickly lock into consistently contributing the full endowment in all remaining rounds, regardless of variation in others’ behavior or obvious antisocial punishment patterns in the group. This shows a strong internal norm for cooperation that is not tightly conditional on others’ contributions or on the presence of “fair” punishment/reward systems. They tolerate the risk that others may exploit their generosity, and they do not scale back their giving in response to either being punished when free-riding or seeing erratic punishment in the group later. They appear to prioritize group efficiency and personal norm-following over payoff maximization, suggesting a cooperative, possibly efficiency-oriented or rule-internalized type rather than a payoff-maximizing conditional cooperator.

This player is a strongly cooperative, mostly unconditional high contributor once the basic incentive structure is clear. They initially match the apparent group norm of full contribution, then briefly experiment with pure free-riding despite ongoing pro‑cooperation chat, and then revert to a near‑always‑max strategy for the rest of the game. After that one deviation and one smaller contribution, they consistently choose the socially efficient contribution level even when others occasionally shirk. They do not visibly condition their later contributions on individual others’ behavior or on the presence/absence of rewards; instead, they appear to lock in a simple, payoff‑dominant heuristic: “always contribute the full amount” (with one later, minor exception). They accept that this sometimes yields lower immediate payoff when a few others free‑ride, suggesting moderate tolerance for inequity as long as the group scheme is mostly intact. Risk-wise, they are willing to expose themselves to exploitation by low contributors for the sake of maintaining a high-output equilibrium, once they have evidence that most players will coordinate on full contribution. Their contributions are stable in the mid‑ and late game, with very low volatility after early experimentation.

The player is a perfectly stable, unconditional full cooperator in this setting. They always choose the maximal contribution available, without any observable adjustment to changing group behavior or payoffs. Their pattern indicates that they prioritize group efficiency over individual payoff, even when others systematically free-ride and when it is clear that contributing yields them strictly lower individual earnings than defecting. They do not display conditional cooperation: their contributions do not track the average contributions of others, nor do they react to declines in group cooperation. This suggests that either (a) they adhere to a strong internal norm of always contributing, or (b) they follow a simple, pre-committed strategy not updated by experience. There is no evidence that they experiment with defection or mixed strategies. They tolerate being exploited by free riders without changing their own behavior. This points toward a fairness or efficiency preference that is not implemented in a reciprocal way—if they care about fairness, they do not use their contribution decision to enforce it. Instead, they behave like a pure cooperator in a one-shot sense, every round. In terms of risk, they accept the “risk” of systematically lower individual payoffs to maintain maximum public good provision. Given the parameters of the game, full contribution is individually irrational but socially optimal, so their behavior is consistent with strong pro-social or rule-following tendencies and low sensitivity to short-term material losses relative to internal principles.

This player is a near-pure, high, stable cooperator. They almost always give the full endowment and only briefly reduce their contribution once when the group’s cooperation level dips. That deviation is modest and immediately reversed, suggesting they treat full contribution as the default norm and only tentatively experiment with lowering it when others pull back. They do not condition their behavior strongly on individual free-riders; one persistently low contributor and occasional partial contributors do not lead them to sustained defection. They seem to prioritize group efficiency and shared payoff over individual short-term gain, and they explicitly value more total earnings over ending the game quickly. They appear comfortable with some exploitation risk: they keep contributing fully despite visible non-contributors and despite having the information needed to chase higher personal profit by reducing their own contributions. Their behavior reflects a fairness norm focused on “everyone should cooperate” rather than “I match each person’s level.” When the group is coordinated and cooperative, they are enthusiastic and supportive; when cooperation frays, they express mild frustration but still behave pro-socially.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator across the entire game. They always contribute their entire endowment to the public good, regardless of others’ behavior, apparent free-riding, or uncertainty about how many rounds remain. They do not appear to condition contributions on past deviations by specific players: even when a peer repeatedly withholds contributions or when some players intermittently defect, this player continues to give the maximum. Their behavior suggests either a strong internal norm of “always cooperate when cooperation is obviously efficient” or a simple heuristic of always contributing when the marginal return to the group is high. They show no evidence of experimenting with defection, even late in the game when others are still occasionally free-riding and when some players explicitly discuss fatigue and unknown game length. They appear comfortable taking on personal risk in the sense of trusting the group, but this is not tested by strong group collapse; cooperation stays high in aggregate. Their fairness standard seems group-focused: they support a cooperative norm for everyone but do not adjust their own contributions to punish or mirror defectors.

This player is a stable “moderate conditional cooperator.” They consistently contribute a medium amount relative to the full endowment and relative to the group, and this amount does not change when others increase their contributions. They are willing to contribute more than a pure free-rider but refrain from fully cooperative levels even when surrounded by many high contributors. This pattern suggests a blend of self-interest and some concern for group benefit, with a tendency to anchor on a personal “fair” contribution level and stick to it. They do not show evidence of strong responsiveness to others’ behavior within the short horizon observed; their contribution does not escalate with rising group contributions, indicating limited conditionality or low sensitivity to social pressure and norms of full cooperation. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they accept some loss of private payoff to support the public good but protect a substantial private portion. Preferences about strict equality vs. efficiency are unclear beyond this moderate stance (low confidence).

This player is a strongly cooperative, norm-focused contributor who usually gives the full endowment to the public fund. Their default is to maintain very high contributions even as others occasionally lower theirs. They only reduce their own contribution a few times, and those reductions are modest relative to full free-riding, suggesting discomfort with deviating too far from a fairness norm rather than a shift to pure self-interest. Their cooperation is conditionally tied to perceived group norms rather than to short-term payoff maximization: they continue to contribute fully even when average contributions dip and when they personally suffer substantial punishment. They do not appear to condition contributions strongly on having received rewards or punishments; contribution levels are remarkably stable despite large shocks. This points to a principled, rule-based cooperation style rather than responsive tit-for-tat. They show mild willingness to shade contributions downward when group averages and some individual contributions fall, but they never exploit very low-contributing peers to take large private gains. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they accept repeated exposure to potential punishment rather than reshaping their contribution behavior to avoid being targeted, implying either confidence that their high contributions are justified or relative indifference to strategic image management via contribution choices alone. Fairness norms are strict and demanding: they seem to view full or near-full contribution as the proper standard for others, and they keep adhering to that themselves even when others deviate. They appear more willing to use the punishment mechanism to enforce norms than to use their own contributions as leverage.

This player is a pure, unwavering free-rider. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute to the public fund, despite consistently benefiting from substantial contributions by others. Their contribution pattern is perfectly stable: it does not shift upward when others contribute more, nor downward when others reduce contributions. There is no evidence of conditional cooperation, reciprocity, or experimentation; they do not test whether increasing their own contributions would change group behavior or payoffs. This suggests a strategy focused on maximizing own payoff within the game’s rules, with no internalized fairness or efficiency norm that would motivate them to sacrifice for the group. They appear highly payoff-focused and risk-averse with respect to giving: they avoid any chance of personal loss from contributing, even when the environment is reliably cooperative and the multiplier is very high. They tolerate receiving higher payoffs than those who contribute more, indicating either indifference to inequity or a belief that such inequity is acceptable within this context. Overall, they behave as a consistent selfish optimizer who treats cooperation as an opportunity to exploit rather than a norm to follow.

This player is a generally high contributor who aims for group cooperation but adjusts contributions in response to perceived free-riding. They contribute well above what a purely self-interested player would, often close to or at the maximum, and rarely choose very low amounts. Their pattern shows conditional cooperation: they sustain generous contributions when others also give, and they scale down when they see repeated zero or very low contributions from several players. They do not, however, retaliate fully by dropping to the minimum; even when others appear selfish, they continue to put in a moderate-to-high amount, suggesting a mixture of pro-social preference and self-protection. Their choices are somewhat volatile but within a band of relatively cooperative behavior rather than swinging between extremes of full defection and full cooperation. They appear to hold an informal fairness norm that others “should” contribute something nontrivial, and they register visible frustration when several players give nothing. They tolerate some inequality but show sensitivity when non-contribution seems systematic. Overall, this is a “conditional high cooperator with mild frustration,” not an unconditional altruist and not a strict payoff maximizer.

This player is a stable, pure free-rider in this environment. They always keep their entire endowment and never contribute to the public fund, despite facing a clearly contribution‑friendly group. Their behavior is highly consistent across rounds, showing no volatility or conditionality with respect to others’ choices. They do not respond cooperatively even when surrounded by very high and increasing contributions from almost all other players, indicating either strong commitment to self-maximizing payoffs or strong skepticism that their own cooperation is worthwhile. They do not exhibit reciprocity or any visible fairness norm; they benefit from others’ generosity without adjusting their own behavior. Risk tolerance in this setting appears low with respect to sacrificing own payoff for collective benefit; they avoid the “risk” of giving up private coins even when the environment is clearly cooperative. Their pattern suggests they either hold a firm belief that free-riding is the rational strategy or they are indifferent to normative expectations of fairness and cooperation in the group. They do not show learning in the cooperative direction; instead, they maintain the same non-contributory strategy despite clear evidence that others are willing to invest heavily in the public good.

This player is a strict, unconditional full contributor. They always allocate their entire endowment to the public fund, with no deviation across the entire game. Their behavior does not track fluctuations in others’ cooperation levels or in personal payoffs; they continue to fully cooperate in both relatively high- and low-contribution environments. They show no evidence of contingent reciprocity, retaliation, or free-riding, even when many others take the endowment for themselves and earn higher individual payoffs. This pattern suggests they prioritize the group-return-maximizing strategy over individual payoff optimization, and they do not adjust based on short-term disadvantage. They appear comfortable with payoff risk in the sense of repeatedly exposing themselves to potential exploitation, but their strategy is actually payoff-dominant at the group level given the high return on contributions. This is consistent with someone who understands (or intuitively accepts) that full contribution can be collectively efficient and chooses that path regardless of others’ behavior. Whether they explicitly compute this is unknown. In normative terms, they act as if there is a firm internal rule to “always contribute everything.” There is no sign that they are trying to match the group average, punish defectors via reduced giving, or experiment strategically. Volatility is effectively zero; the contribution rule is simple, rigid, and stable.

This player is a stable, unconditional full contributor. They always give their entire endowment to the public good and never deviate, regardless of others’ occasional defections, the availability of punishment/reward, or the game’s progression. Their strategy indicates a strong internal norm that full cooperation is the “correct” action, not a contingent response to others’ behavior. They do not appear to experiment with lower contributions to test the group or to exploit defectors, suggesting low interest in strategic free-riding and high concern for maintaining maximum group efficiency. Given that contributing is individually costly but socially efficient, their behavior is consistent with a pro-social, norm-driven or rule-following type rather than a narrowly payoff-maximizing one. Risk tolerance concerning exploitation is high: they continue to contribute fully even when others defect and even when those defectors are not perfectly controlled by punishment.

This player is a strict, unconditionally full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment, with no deviation, regardless of how others behave or how payoffs compare. Their behavior is extremely stable and shows no sign of experimentation, strategic adjustment, or conditionality. They do not appear to use standard reciprocity rules such as “match the group” or “punish defectors by reducing my own contribution.” Even when others free-ride persistently and repeatedly earn higher payoffs, this player maintains maximal contribution. This indicates that fairness considerations, if present, are not translating into retaliatory behavior or self-protective defection; instead, they prioritize contributing to the public pool over avoiding exploitation. Their pattern fits what is often described in the literature as a “moralistic” or “principled” cooperator who treats contribution as the right thing to do independent of returns. They behave as if guided by a rule like “I always contribute fully,” ignoring short-term incentives and the fact that the marginal per-capita return is below 1 for an individual. This suggests low sensitivity to payoff maximization in favor of a strong internal norm. Risk tolerance appears high in the sense that they repeatedly accept the risk (and reality) of being exploited by free-riders. They show no evidence of hedging by occasionally keeping their endowment. At the same time, their behavior is not exploratory; once they adopt full contribution, they stick with it, suggesting a low appetite for strategic risk-taking or optimization but a high tolerance for payoff risk in service of their chosen norm. They do not appear to condition their contribution on group efficiency either. Their cooperation persists through phases of both low and high total contributions by others. When others around them fluctuate between free-riding and cooperating, they do not reduce or increase their own contributions in response; they simply maintain maximum giving throughout. This constancy points to a rule-based, identity-consistent strategy rather than outcome-contingent reasoning. Overall, this player is a highly stable, norm-driven full cooperator who does not adapt contributions to others’ actions, group behavior, or payoff patterns. They place intrinsic value on contributing and behave as if contribution is obligatory rather than optional or negotiable.

The player is a stable full cooperator under the given binary choice structure. They consistently contribute the maximum allowed amount regardless of others’ past deviations. Their behavior indicates a strong efficiency norm: they act as if the cooperative outcome is the default, even when free-riding is present. There is no observable conditional reduction in contribution in response to low contributors, suggesting they prioritize maintaining the cooperative standard over adjusting to exploiters. Risk tolerance appears moderate to high in social terms: they are willing to expose themselves to being exploited rather than hedge by withholding contributions. Fairness for them is enforced primarily via punishment rather than via changing their own contribution level.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this setting. They always contribute the maximum allowed amount, even after observing that some others free ride and that high contributors can be targeted with punishment. Their behavior suggests a strong internal norm favoring full contribution rather than fine-tuned conditional cooperation. They do not visibly adjust to fluctuations in others’ behavior or to the existence of costly punishment and reward technologies; they appear willing to accept personal risk or disadvantage in order to maintain a fully cooperative stance. Fairness for them seems defined as “everyone putting in the maximum,” and they personally adhere to that norm regardless of others’ deviations. Risk tolerance around exploitation is relatively high: they continue to expose themselves to potential punishment and to potential losses from others’ under-contribution without hedging.

This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They consistently give their entire endowment to the public fund every time, with no evidence of conditioning on others’ past behavior. They maintain this even after seeing that full contribution can lead to a worse personal payoff when others free-ride or when they themselves are punished. Their behavior aligns with a strong efficiency and fairness norm: they appear to regard full contribution as the “right” or expected action, regardless of others’ deviations. There is no sign of strategic withholding, experimentation, or risk-hedging; they accept the risk of exploitation rather than under-contribute. Overall, they fit a “principled cooperator” type rather than a payoff-maximizer.

This player is primarily a non-contributor with sporadic, seemingly strategic full contributions. Their default is to keep the entire endowment and free-ride on others’ contributions whenever possible. Full contributions occur only in a minority of rounds and do not show a consistent, sustained cooperative phase; they appear as tactical experiments or short-lived shifts rather than a stable norm. Contribution decisions are weakly conditional on others’ behavior but not in a straightforward reciprocity pattern. The player sometimes contributes fully when the group (or a large subset) is also contributing fully, suggesting awareness that mutual cooperation yields high payoffs. However, they also frequently defect even when several others are clearly cooperating and maintaining contributions. They do not show strong fairness or equality concerns in their contribution choices. They are comfortable taking the private benefit of defection even when others are bearing the cost of cooperation. Risk tolerance in contribution is moderate: they occasionally “risk” contributing in mixed environments, but quickly revert to defection rather than trying to stabilize cooperation through their own behavior. Overall, their type on contribution is a mostly opportunistic free-rider with intermittent cooperative actions that do not blossom into a sustained conditional-cooperator pattern.

The player is a predominantly selfish non-contributor with occasional, strategic full contributions. Their baseline is to keep the entire endowment and free-ride on whatever others provide. They do not gradually adjust; instead, their contribution pattern is bimodal: almost always zero with a few isolated full contributions. Those few full contributions occur when nearly everyone else is contributing, suggesting they are willing to cooperate only when cooperation is already strong and likely to be rewarded via higher payoffs. They do not appear to follow a fairness norm such as “match others’ contributions” or “contribute when others are kind,” because they frequently defect while others contribute substantial amounts. They show low concern for social efficiency: they repeatedly choose the individually profitable but collectively inefficient action even when they can see that group contributions generate higher total returns. Risk tolerance around contribution is asymmetric: they avoid being an early or lone cooperator and only contribute fully when it is “safe” (many others are already contributing), then quickly revert back to defection. Overall, they behave as an opportunistic free-rider with occasional tactical cooperation rather than a stable cooperator or conditional cooperator.

This player is a selective, payoff-conscious conditional cooperator with moderate volatility. They alternate between full contribution and full free-riding rather than mixing intermediate levels (which are not available), suggesting a binary, strategic framing of choices. Early in the game, they quickly shift from initial free-riding to frequent full contributions as they observe substantial group giving, indicating responsiveness to others’ behavior and a willingness to support a cooperative norm when it seems established. They are not rigidly cooperative, however: after several cooperative choices, they temporarily revert to free-riding while others continue to contribute, revealing that maximizing personal gain can override consistency with the emerging norm. Their behavior suggests they track relative payoffs and recognize that contributing is individually costly given the low marginal per-capita return; they still contribute often, implying some weight on group efficiency or fairness, but they intermittently exploit cooperators when it is safe. Their final pattern mixes cooperation and defection rather than converging strictly to one, which points to a flexible, opportunistic style rather than a fixed moral rule. Risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to contribute even when some defectors remain, but they also hedge by occasionally defecting despite ongoing cooperation by others. Overall, they are best described as a pragmatic conditional cooperator with opportunistic deviations.

This player is a consistently high contributor who gravitates toward near‑full cooperation. After an initial moderate contribution, they quickly move to giving their full endowment and maintain that level in almost all subsequent rounds, including the very last one. A single mid‑game reduction appears context‑driven rather than a shift in norm; they immediately return to full contributions afterward. Their behavior suggests a strong fairness norm: they keep contributing fully even when one group member repeatedly gives much less and is heavily punished, indicating that their own standard of “doing the right thing” is relatively unconditional and not tightly tied to matching others’ exact levels. They tolerate some exploitation and do not visibly use their contribution level as a retaliatory tool. Risk tolerance is medium–high: they are willing to invest heavily in the public good without clear assurance of stable cooperation from everyone, relying on the overall cooperative climate rather than finely tuning to each defector.

This player is a generally high contributor with intermittent, strategic free-riding. They frequently give the full endowment, especially early and late, indicating a baseline cooperative or norm-following inclination.
